Maximum marks Professional marks Analysis and evaluation Appropriate assessment of the issues raised in relation to Geller Co's going concern situation,using examples where relevant to support overall comments - Identification of omissions from the analysis with an effective appraisal of the information to make suitable recommendations for appropriate courses of action Professional scepticism and professional judgement - Effective challenge and critical assessment of how management has prepared the cash flow forecast,with appropriate conclusions Balanced assessment and use of professional judgement of the information to conclude upon the appropriate audit opinion in the circumstances Commercial acumen Appropriate recognition of the wider implications on the engagement,the audit firm and the company Maximum marks Maximum 5 Answers Strategic Professional-Options,AAA-INT Advanced Audit and Assurance-International(AAA-INT) March/June 2023 Sample Answers 1 Briefing notes To:Isabella Phoenix,audit engagement partner From:Audit manager Subject:Audit planning in relation to Encore Co Date:1 July 20X5 Introduction These briefing notes have been prepared to assist in planning the audit of our new audit client,Encore Co.The notes begin with an evaluation of the significant risks of material misstatement which should be considered in planning the audit.The notes then focus on inventory as a balance giving rise to a significant risk of material misstatement and include relevant audit procedures.Finally,thee notes discuss the ethical issues arising from two requests from the client for our firm to provide additional services to Encore Co. (a)Risks of material misstatement Materiality For the purposes of these briefing notes,the following overall materiality level will be used to assess the significance of identified risks and as requested,this has been based on the company's revenue. Benchmark based on revenue as per audit engagement partner's instruction: 1-2%of revenue =range of $49,000-$98,000. This benchmark is only a starting point for determining materiality and professional judgement will need to be applied in determining a final level to be applied during the course of the audit.Based on these preliminary figures,a materiality level of $49,000 has been used in preparing these briefing notes. Materiality is based on 1-2%of revenue and is determined at the lower end of the range due to the size of the company and due to it being a new client and ensuring that adequate audit evidence is obtained where areas of judgement in management estimates affect the financial statements. Tutorial note: Credit will also be awarded for justifiable alternative benchmark percentages,e.g.0·5-1%. Payroll There are several risks of material misstatement relating to payroll,which is a highly material class of transaction within thee financial statements.First,the finance director has indicated that internal controls were deficient in respect of payroll,which is concerning given the significance of these costs.Problems such as missing timesheets could mean that payroll is understated, and lack of authorisation could indicate errors in the amount of overtime claimed.Possibly,employees have been claiming additional overtime which has not actually been worked,knowing that the overtime payments are not going to be scrutinised before payment. Both of these issues mean that operating profit for the first eight months of the financial year could be over or understated,and there are implications for inventory valuation,which willbe discussed in part (b)of these briefing notes. From 1 June 20X5,Bear Payroll Services(BPS)is providing a payroll service to Encore Co.Outsourcing does have an impact on audit planning.ISA 402 Audit Considerations Relating to an Entity using a Service Organisation requires the auditor to obtain an understanding of how the audited entity uses the services of a service organisation in order to evaluate the risk of material misstatement.It will be particularly important for Garrick &Co to understand exactly what services are being provided by BPS and their relevant internal controls.The design and implementation of relevant controls at Encore Co must also be evaluated,for example,whether the transactions and information provided by BPS are monitored and whether checks are performed prior to inclusion of information relating to payroll in the financial statements. Given the history of weak controls and the small size of the finance department at Encore Co,the outsourcing of payroll may reduce the risk of material misstatement,but this needs to be evaluated after further evaluation of BPS and their arrangements with Encore Co. Impairment The company's head office was damaged in a recent storm and it is anticipated that its insurance policy will cover the repair of this property.While the financial impact of the damage is not yet quantified,the property's carrying amount is material at $108,000.The accounting for these events will need to be carefully considered as there is a risk that assets and profit are overstated if the damage and subsequent insurance claim have not been accounted for correctly. The physical damage caused to the head office is an indicator of impairment which should be recorded in line with IAS®36 Impairment of Assets.Management should conduct an impairment review,to determine the recoverable amount of the property and whether any impairment loss should be recognised.Assets other than the property could have been damaged and the impairment loss could be greater than just the amount attributable to the property.The risk is that assets are overstated,and profit overstated,if any necessary impairment of assets is not recognised at the reporting date. IAS 16 Property,Plant and Equipment requires the impairment and derecognition of property,plant and equipment and any subsequent compensation claims to be treated as separate economic events and accounted for separately in the period they Occur. The standard specifically states that it is not appropriate to net the events off and not record an impairment loss because there is an insurance claim in relation to the same assets.As such,this may mean that Encore Co has to account for the impairment loss in the current year but should recognise the compensation claim when it becomes receivable.If the insurance company has not yet confirmed that they will pay $110,000 in settlement of the insurance claim,then receivables and deferred incomee are overstated by a material amount. Joint arrangement Encore Co has entered into a joint arrangement with EIm Associates and the company intends to recognise an intangible asset of $75,000,which is material to the financial statements,in respect of their investment into this arrangement. The amount invested should be accounted for under IFRS®11 Joint Arrangements.A joint arrangement is an arrangement whereby two or more parties have joint control and to which the parties are bound by a contractual arrangement.Thee development project seems to meet the definition of a joint operation,which is defined in IFRS 11 as an arrangement whereby the parties,having joint control of the arrangement,have rights to the assets,and also obligations for the liabilities.In addition, a joint operation is not structured through a separate vehicle,which is the case in this situation. Under the requirements of IFRS 11,each party to the joint arrangement recognises its share of the assets,liabilities,revenues and expenses of the joint arrangement,with the share determined based on the rights and obligations of each party as set out in the contractual terms. However,it is doubtful that the $75,000 meets the definition and recognition criteria within IAS 38 Intangible Assets to be capitalised.An intangible asset can only be recognised if a separately identifiable asset is created,which is controlled and capable of generating future economic benefit.From the information provided,it does not appear that an asset will have been created by the year end,as the relevant definitions are not met,nor have the criteria for recognition of any of the costs as a development intangible asset.The finance director will need to provide specific evidence to justify the recognition of any of this amount as an intangible asset,e.g.details of the products being developed,forecasts indicating the future economic benefit to be generated and demonstration of commercial viability and technical feasibility. The part of the $75,000 which relates to payroll costs of the two engineers can be allocated to the joint arrangement and accounted for as an expense under IFRS 11,but the element relating to 'intellectual property'does not seem to have any basis for inclusion as a cost or investment into the joint arrangement. If the finance director insists on their proposed accounting treatment,intangible assets will be overstated and payroll expenses understated for that element of the $75,000. Tutorial note: The finance director has not specified where the credit side of the entry would be posted in relation to the 'intellectual property',so it is not possible to comment on this. Finally,the proposed accounting treatment is clearly in contravention of IFRS®Accounting Standards,and therefore the competency of the finance director should be questioned.This in itself creates a potential risk of material misstatement,and other accounting treatments may need to be challenged. Historic boat At $50,000,the value of the historic boat,which is based on the finance director's valuation,is material to the financial statements.The finance director's proposed accounting treatment is incorrect because the historic boat does not meet thee definition of property,plant and equipment.According to IAS 16,property,plant and equipment is defined as tangible items which are held for use in the production or supply of goods or services,for rental to others,or for administrative purposes;and are expected to be used during more than one period.The boat is on display at the company headquarters but is not being used by the company to generate revenue or in the production of goods,therefore,it cannot be classified as property,plant and equipment. The boat could be recognised as an asset,assuming that a reliable value for it can be found,because even if it is not being used in a productive capacity,it would have a realisable value should it be disposed of in the future.Therefore,the economic benefitit could generate is based on its fair value. The risk of misstatement relates to how the asset is classified within the financial statements,and whether a reliable fair value can be found.The finance director's valuation is probably not reliable,and an expert should be sought to provide a valuation for the asset. When the fair value of the historic boat has been reliably determined,the boat can be recognised as an asset at that fair value. However,the credit side of the entry should not be recognised within income.This is because the boat has been gifted from a shareholder,and the Conceptual Framework excludes contributions from shareholders from the definition of income. Instead,the credit should be recognised in equity as a capital contribution from a shareholder.Based on the finance director's proposed accounting treatment,profit is overstated,and equity understated,by $50,000,and further adjustments may be necessary depending on the results of the expert's valuation of the historic boat. Analytical review The company's revenue is forecast to increase by 5·3%this year,but there is a much larger projected increase in operating profit of 22·5%.The operating margin is forecast to increase from 10·3%to 12%.Unless the trend in profit can be explained by valid business reasons,it could indicate understatement of operating expenses or overstatement of revenue.This could possibly be linked to the deficiencies in internal controls,i.e.missing timesheets,which could mean that some expenses have gone unrecorded,though this is unlikely to explain such a large reduction in operating expenses on its own. New audit client There is a risk that opening balances and comparative information may not be correct.While the previous auditor's opinions have been unmodified for the last six years,the risk of misstatement is elevated in relation to payroll costs,as internal controls were known to be weak in this area prior to the appointment of BPS to provide a payrollservice to Encore Co.If internal controls are found to be deficient in other areas,then the risk of misstatement in opening balances and comparatives increases. Because the prior year figures were not audited by Garick &Co,we should plan to audit the opening balances carefully,in accordance with ISA 510 Initial Audit Engagements-Opening Balances,to ensure that opening balances and comparative information are both free from material misstatement. Conclusion In conclusion,there are several significant risks of material misstatement.Some are at the financial statement level,for example, the possible lack of competence of the new finance director,and some at the assertion level,for example,the recognition and measurement of impaired assets and associated provisions.Other,less pervasive,risks of material misstatement are also present,for example,in relation to the accounting treatment which is proposed in relation to the historic boat.The control environment within the company is also called into question due to the internal control deficiencies noted by the finance director,though this may be mitigated through the use of a service organisation.Our audit plan will need to be robust with respect to all of the matters described in this section of the briefing notes. (b)Inventory Justification In total,the inventories balance has increased by 17%,and the balance in total is highly material to the financial statements. The individual categories of work in progress and finished goods are material in their own right. Raw materials have increased by 50%,and this increase has been explained by a recent bulk purchase,which seems to be a reasonable explanation,though it should be corroborated by audit procedures. Work in progress has increased by 10·0%,which seems high when compared with an increase in revenue of 5·4%.Estimating the value of work in progress is complicated and involves the use of management judgement.The fact that Gavin Bee is a new finance director elevates this risk as he may be unfamiliar with the methods used to value work in progress relating to boats, leading to inappropriate estimates of valuation being made.He may also take a different approach to that used previously, resulting in a lack of consistency in how work in progress is valued. In addition,internal control deficiencies were found by the new finance director which means that costs may not be properly allocated to contracts and the manual inputting of data gives rise to a high risk of error.The allocation of overheads on a percentage basis may not be an accurate reflection of the actual production costs incurred.In addition,there may be somee contracts which are incuring higher than anticipated costs,and could be loss-making,in which case a provision should be recognised. No information has been provided by the client regarding how management evaluates the profitability of contracts and there is a risk that the process is not happening at all,or that the process is not robust enough to identify potentially loss-making contracts. For the finished goods,the boat which is being stored for a customer should not be included in inventory if the company has completed its contractual obligation.The boat has received its licence to sail but has not yet been delivered to the customer. If Encore Co has fufilled its contractual obligations,a sale should be recognised and the boat derecognised from inventory. Storing the boat for several months before the customer can take delivery is not a valid reason to continue to recognise the boat as inventory. The boat which has not passed its safety inspection could be overvalued if Encore Co has to incur further costs to ensure that the boat passes the inspection.IAS 2 Inventories requires inventories to be valued at the lower of cost and net realisable value, and it is possible that further costs could reduce the net realisable value of the boat to below its cost,in which case a reduction in value should be recognised. Procedures For the raw materials: - Agree a sample of items included in raw materials to supplier invoice and purchase order placed with the supplier to confirm the cost of the items and their existence. Obtain and review the bulk purchase discount offered by the supplier and agree that specific transaction to cash book and bank statement and to goods received notes and trace into the inventory system. For the work in progress: - For the contracts included in work in progress,agree a sample of direct costs to supporting documentation,e.g.raw materials to suppler invoices,payroll costs to timesheets and payroll records. - Obtain the calculations detailing the method of overhead allocation and review for appropriateness,e.g.to ensure that only production related overheads are included in the overhead allocation. - Re-perform the work in progress calculation,e.g.confirm the correct percentage has been applied for overhead allocation. - From a review of contract cost accounts and comparison with contract prices,discussion with management and production supervisors,determine whether any contracts could be loss making - Visit the boat building facility at the year end to form an auditor's expectation of the stage of completion of each boat,and follow up in the final audit,to ensure that the work in progress of each contract appears reasonable. For the finished goods: - For the boat which has not passed its safety inspection,obtain the inspector's report and discuss with relevant personnel, e.g.quality control manager,to establish the reason for the failure to obtain the licence. Discuss with relevant personnel to obtain understanding of the work needed to rectify the problem and the costs involved. Based on the above,determine whether the additional costs will impact on the lower of cost and net realisable value of the boat. - Consider the impact of events after the year end on the valuation of the finished goods,e.g.confirm that the boat is delivered to the customer in October 20X5 and that the sale is recognised at that point in time. For the boat being stored at the dockyard,confirm through a review of supporting documentation,e.g.customer account and invoices raised,whether the sale of the boat has been accounted for. Review the terms and conditions of the contract to determine when Encore Co completes its obligations under the contract and therefore whether the sale should be recognised and the inventory derecognised. (c)Ethical issues The finance director is correct that internal controls will be evaluated as part of the audit,but he is requesting an additional service.Therefore,it should be arranged as an engagement separate to the audit,with a separate engagement letter detailing the scope of the work which is required.There should be a fee separate to the audit fee;the separate review of internal controls should not be included within the audit fee. The IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (the Code)suggests that providing this type of work for an audit client,as an engagement separate from the audit,could be considered as providing an internal audit service.Thee Code states that internal audit services involve assisting the client in the monitoring of internal controls by reviewing controls, monitoring their operation and recommending improvements to them. Reviewing the internal controls of an audit client which are relevant to the financial reporting system would create a self-review threat as the auditor would consequently assess the effectiveness of the control system during the external audit.It is possible that the audit team may use the results of the internal audit service for the purposes of the audit without appropriately evaluating the results or exercising an appropriate level of professional scepticism. To safeguard the sel-review threat,professionals who are not members of the audit team should perform the review of internal controls. In addition,the design,implementation and maintenance of internal controls are management responsibilities.The Code states that taking responsibility for designing,implementing monitoring and maintaining internal control is an example of where an audit firm assumes the responsibility of management. Encore Co is not a listed entity,so there is not a prohibition on the provision of an internal audit service to the client.If Garrick &Co provides the review of internal controls,in order to avoid assuming a management responsibility,they should be satisfied that the management team of Encore Co: - Acknowledges their responsibility for designing,implementing,monitoring and maintaining internal control. - Reviews,assesses and approves the scope,risk and frequency of the internal audit service. - Evaluates the internal audit service provided and the findings of the service including determining which recommendations should be implemented. - Manages the implementation of the recommendations. Garrick &Co should request written acknowledgement of the matters indicated above as part of their evaluation as to whether to provide this additional engagement to Encore Co. In relation to the loan guarantee,providing a guarantee represents a financial interest in an audit client.It would create a sel-interest threat because the audit firm has an interest in the financial position of the client,causing loss of objectivity when auditing the financial statements. According to the Code,if an audit firm guarantees a loan to an audit client,the self-interest threat created would be so significant that no safeguards could reduce the threat to an acceptable level unless the loan or guarantee is immaterial to both the audit firm and the client. In this case,the loan would be material and the request to provide the loan guarantee should be declined. 2 (a)Client acceptance When determining whether to enter into a professional relationship with a new client,an assurance firm is required to assess whether this would be possible under the criteria of ISQM1Quality Management for Firms that Perform Audits or Reviews of Financial Statements or other Assurance or Related Service Engagements. This means Gloop &Co shallmake professional judgements based on the following quality objectives regarding whether to accept the engagement: - Information obtained about the nature and circumstances of the engagement; - Consideration of the integrity and ethical values of the client;and - The firm's ability to perform the engagement in accordance with professional standards and applicable legal and regulatory requirements. In addition to these acceptance judgements,Gloop &Co should also ensure they are following the IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (the Code). Integrity and ethical values of the client Gloop &Co has already performed client identification procedures,however,they will sill be required to perform other elements of customer due diligence such as considering the business reputation of Slaindar Co's principal owners,key management,and those charged with governance.Consideration should be made by the audit team regarding whether there are any indications that the client might be involved in money laundering or other criminal activities. The firm will aso need to consider management's reasons for appointing a different firm from its auditors and the potential for management bias in the preparation of the cash flow forecast in support of the application for finance.It is possible that approaching Gloop&Co to perform this assignment and potentially the audit in future is an intimidation threat to the existing auditors whereby they act in accordance with the client demands to avoid a loss of income.ISQM1 suggests the firm should consider the reason for not using the existing auditors when assessing the integrity and ethical values of the client. To provide an assurance report on forecasts such as this,a good understanding of the client and its business is required,and the current audit firm will usually have the requisite knowledge and understanding.Gloop &Co should therefore consider whether the use of a different firm creates a risk that the client may be hoping that Gloop &Co lacks the client knowledge to effectively challenge the key assumptions underlying the preparation of the forecast.When a professional accountant is asked to perform work for a non-audit client,they should be given permission by the client to contact its auditors to obtain relevant information.If this permission is not given,the appointment should be declined. In addition,Gloop&Co should consider the attitude of the client and the risk of bias.The proposal by Aaron that funds are raised to purchase the right to operate under his name may not result in a separately identifiable intangible asset with a value of $1 million,given the external valuation of $100,000.It is also possible that the company would be able to continue to trade under the existing name without the purchase'.The proposal may lack sufficient commercial substance.The way Aaron has described the proposal for the business plan could be considered potentially fraudulent in the manner Aaron proposes to describe the transaction to the bank.Aaron's comment that 'the bank does not need to know the brand is his own name' suggests a significant integrity issue and Gloop&Co should not accept Slaindar Co as a client if Aaron is proposing withholding information from the bank. Additionally,there is a risk that the forecast might be manipulated to ensure the highest possibility of the bank providing finance or management may pressure Gloop &Co to present a favourable opinion regarding the forecast due to Aaron's desire to retire and extract funds on doing so. Performance of the engagement in accordance with professional,legal,and regulatory standards It would be expected as a firm providing audit and related services that Gloop &Co has staff within the firm who have appropriate competence and capabilities to perform the assurance on the cash flow forecast.However,the short deadline of 30 September 20X5 may limit the resources,in respect of both the availability of staff and being able to obtain suficient evidence,which may put pressure on the firm to perform the work in the time scale required.This might impact on the quality of the engagement which can be performed.Where this is the case,Gloop&Co should not accept the engagement. Ethical issues Given that Slaindar Co is not currently a client of Gloop &Co,the firm's independence from Slaindar Co will not have been previously considered.In this regard,it is important to ensure there are no threats to the firm's objectivity which might prevent it from accepting the appointment.If the firm is not independent and its objectivity is compromised,the reliability of thee assurance report will be undermined. It appears Aaron is encouraging Gloop &Co to accept the review engagement by offering the audit appointment as an incentive, on the assumption that the outcome of the loan application is successful.This creates a self-interest threat in that Gloop&Co has a financial interest in accepting Slaindar Co as a client in performing the review engagement to secure a future appointment as the company's auditor. There is an additional self-interest threat arising from the offer of free food and beverages for life.The Code prohibits thee acceptance of hospitality unless the value is trivial,and the offer does not constitute an inducement toward a particular outcome.Here,the offer has been made as an inducement to ensure a positive assurance review and the value over a lifetime is likely to be significant,so this cannot be accepted.The inducement could be construed as a bribe and an indicator that Aaron's integrity is in doubt. An advocacy threat is also created because Gloop &Co has an incentive to promote the financial interests of Slaindar Co to thee bank to ensure the loan willbe provided,and this may impact the quality and objectivity of the review engagement. Gloop&Co should consider whether any safeguards can be implemented to reduce any ethical threats to an acceptable level, for example,using an independent second partner to review the work performed for the review engagement.If safeguards do not reduce the threats to an acceptable level,then the review engagement should not be performed. Gloop&Co should also consider whether there are any conflicts of interest between Slaindar Co and any of the firm's existing clients.Where any such conflicts exist,Gloop &Co would be required to notify both Slaindar Co and the existing client explaining the situation and proposed safeguards which will be implemented to mitigate the risk.Permission in writing will be required from both parties to proceed with the engagement. Conclusion Given the issues with respect to client integrity and the high risk arising from bank reliance,it is likely Gloop&Co would choose to reject the assurance work. (b)Sales Assumptions with respect to sales appear to be very optimistic.There is no guarantee that levels of sales at each coffee shop will return to the pre-downturn values as rapidly as forecast.New coffe shops appear to be expected to generate the same level of sales from the beginning of the period in which they are set up.This is unlikely for two reasons:first,the coffee shops are unlikely to be open for the full six months and will take time to become successful and established operations.Second,these coffee shops are in smaller airports which are unlikely to have the same level of fotfall as the coffee shops in larger airports. This means a lower level of sales would be expected from the newly opened shops. The sales figure per coffee shop included by management appears to show growth of 28%over the period of the forecast.As the figures are all based on average sales levels per restaurant in 20X3,growth in sales is expected to increase only by price inflation.Although the level of inflation expected in the first 12 months of the forecast has been assumed by the preparer, the growth in sales per restaurant from year ending 30 June 20X6 to the year ending 30 June 20X8 is 28%which appears questionable.Based on 10%inflation per annum,this is expected to be 21%over two-year period.At 10%p.a.,sale price inflation already appears to be unrealistic and as such,revenues are likely to be overstated. Payments to suppliers Payments to suppliers assumes a gross margin of 70%,therefore costs of sales at 30%.This may be representative of historical levels but further information will be needed in order to determine if this is reasonable for this industry. Payments appear to be lagging sales by an entire six-month period.Such a credit period from suppliers is unlikely,especially given the current difficulties with the main supplier requesting cash on delivery Given the above,the timing of the cash flows to suppliers is likely to be incorrect and potentially also understated. Lease payments Average lease costs for the existing 20 coffee shop locations are $51,250 for a six-month period giving a total cost each period of $1,025,000 as at the start of the forecast period.The lease cost of the 25 shops at the end of the forecast is $1,185,000 which,after removing the lease costs of the original coffe shops,gives lease costs of $160,000 for the five new shops.This gives a total value of lease costs for the new coffee shops at only $32,000 per location.This may be due to the locations,with smaller airports charging lower rentals or because Aaron has been able to use his contacts to negotiate low prices,however, these costs should be scrutinised carefully to ensure lease costs are not understated. Other costs including staff Other costs appear to represent a constant $25,000 per coffee shop for each six-month period.This is unrealistic as there are likely to be inflationary increases in costs and it is expected some staff would receive pay rises over the course of the next three years.This results in an understatement of costs. Capital expenditure Capital expenditure has been included for the new locations,however,no ongoing capital expenditure appears to be planned for the existing locations.It is likely some additional capital expenditure would be required for existing premises and therefore this figure should be higher and present in more than the two periods currently included. Marketing The marketing costs appear to be very low at only $20,000.This may be because customers at airports have little choice available and competition is low,however,it is likely that even within an airport there is an element of choice,and some marketing is needed to attract customers to Slaindar Co's coffee shops. Marketing appears to only be planned for the six-month period to 31 December 20X6.It would be expected marketing costs would be recurring.It appears marketing outflows may be understated. Loans and interest The forecast assumes the bank will provide financing for the expansion and as such,it is reasonable to include the receipt of loan finance in the forecast.Loan repayments for the loan appear to have been omitted,with loan repayment outflows remaining constant,presumably relating to the opening loan position. There is also no increase in interest payments over the period.This is not in line with expectation as additional interest would arise on both the new loan and on the new leased properties.As such,interest is understated. Tax There does not appear to be an outflow in the forecast for tax payments.This may be due to the company utilising losses experienced in the prior two years,however,this assumption should be further investigated to ensure tax payments are not understated. Conclusion The forecast appears to be based on very optimistic assumptions which may be inappropriate in the current economic climate and has several omissions which should also beinvestigated. 3 (a)(i) Matters to be included in respect of deficiencies in internal control In each case,the auditor needs to use judgement to determine whether the deficiency in internal control which has been identified is significant enough to warrant disclosure.According to ISA 265 Communicating Deficiencies in Internal Control to Those Charged with Governance and Management,the significance of a deficiency or a combination of deficiencies in internal control depends not only on whether a misstatement has actually occurred,but also on the likelihood that a misstatement could occur and the potential magnitude of the misstatement. New payroll system The audit procedures have identified a deficiency in that internal controls over systems changes have not been adequate. When a new system is introduced,there should be a range of controls to ensure that data transfer is complete and accurate.It is common that old and new systems operate in parallel for a period of time so that problems can be quickly identified and rectified.The system change was clearly not well planned or performed,and this could indicate a wider weakness in the general controls within the organisation. It is likely that the payroll system generates figures which are material to the financial statements.Even though audit procedures have indicated that there is no related material misstatement,a lack of controls over payroll could result in errors in the calculation and payment of payroll taxes,which in turn could lead to fines or penalties if mistakes are made. This potential problem should be communicated,as well as recommendations for establishing the internal controls which should be in place when there are significant changes to accounting systems. Marketing director The marketing director acquired a new laptop computer immediately before leaving the company,and the laptop computer has not been returned to Violet Co.Even though the laptop computer is not a significant asset,it remains on the company's non-current asset register,and having been paid for by Violet Co,it should have been given back to the company.If the marketing director deliberately acquired the laptop computer using company funds but with no intention of reimbursing the company or returning the asset on leaving,this amounts to a theft of company assets,which appears to have been endorsed by the financial controller.The behaviour of the financial controller questions their integrity and its overall effect on the risk profile of the audit client. Also,the marketing director may still have access to the company's servers and remote access to the company's financial information and bank accounts.If this is the case,there may have been other acts of misappropriation,e.g.theft of cash from the company's bank account,with implications for the integrity of the financial statements. This matter should be communicated to management and those charged with governance despite the fact that the value of the laptop is not likely to be significant to the company or material to the financial statements.ISA 265 identifies that the identification of management fraud,whether or not material,which was not prevented by the entity's internal control, is an indicator of a significant deficiency in internal controls which should be communicated. However,it is possible that the marketing director has reimbursed Violet Co,in which case the laptop should be removed from the non-current asset register. (ii)Matters to be communicated to those charged with governance Delays in providing information to the audit team Those charged with governance should be made aware of the delay which the audit team encountered in receiving important information.According to ISA 260 Communication with Those Charged with Governance,significant delays by management,the unavailability of entity personnel,or an unwillingness by management to provide information necessary for the auditor to perform the auditor's procedures represent significant difficulties encountered during the audit,which should be communicated. The information underpinning the trial balance should have been prepared by the finance director and made available to the audit team but it had not been prepared as requested.The information was eventually received,but the delay will have meant that the audit did not run as eficiently as planned.The extra work may have implications for the audit fee, which should also be discussed. Controls over manual journals There appears to be control deficiencies or simply a lack of documentation over manual journals.Crawley &Co should recommend that controls are strengthened over the posting of manual journals,to both reduce the risk of material misstatement in the financial statements and to avoid additional audit work being necessary next year. It is particularly concerning that the same issue arose last year.Crawley &Co should raise the issue with those charged with governance in order to understand why last year's discussion has not been acted upon. Change in accounting policy This planned change in accounting policy could have a significant impact on the company's financial statements. ISA 260 suggests that communication with those charged with governance may include a discussion of the qualitative aspects of accounting practices,including any changes in significant accounting policies.Crawley &Co may wish to explain in their communication the potential impact on the financial statements if such a policy were adopted.Crawley &Co should consider if Violet Co has a motivation to include properties at fair value in order to present the statement of financial position in a more favourable light. The audit firm may also comment on the key aspects of the accounting policy,for example,the ways in which fair value can be established,and the need for monitoring movements in fair value so that subsequent revaluation gains and losses can be properly identified and accounted for. In addition,Crawley &Co may also wish to discuss the practical implications of this policy,such as the cost of external valuations.The audit firm could also discuss the changes which may be needed in the audit approach for properties, when the policy is implemented in the next financial year. Board meetings Those charged with governance should be made aware of the finance director's request for the audit engagement partner to attend monthly board meetings.There is nothing to prohibit an auditor from simply attending the board meetings of an audit client as an observer.There may be circumstances in which a representative from the audit firm needs to attend, for example,in order to inform those charged with governance of significant issues affecting auditor independence. However,the auditor attending the client's board meeting must be careful that they take no part in any management decisions made at the meeting.If matters not relevant to the audit are debated on which the auditor's opinion is sought, the auditor could be deemed to be involved with management decisions,or to be providing an additional service to the client which potentially creates a threat to objectivity. The IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants states that if an auditor serves as a director or officer of an audit client,the self-review and self-interest threats created would be so significant that no safeguards could reduce the threats to an acceptable level.Accordingly,no partner oremployee should be appointed to the board of an audit client. These points should be explained to those charged with governance so that they understand why it was inappropriate for the finance director to make this request,and why the audit firm should decline. (b)Branson Co-critique of auditor's report of going concern There is a significant uncertainty over going concern,and the issue should not be included in key audit matters(KAM). Instead,it should be disclosed in a separate section of the auditor's report entitled Material uncertainty related to going concern (MURGC)in accordance with ISA 570 Going Concern.This is only appropriate if the audit team has suficient,appropriate audit evidence to reach the conclusion that there is significant doubt over going concern,which has been adequately disclosed in the financial statements in accordance with IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements which is the situation in this scenario. The description of the company's loss-making status is too vague and from the audit evidence it seems that the company's cashh flow problems are the reason for its going concern problems,so it would be more appropriate to mention cash flow problems rather than losses in the MURGC paragraph. There is reference to where the going concern problems are discussed in the company's operating and financial review.The auditor's report should refer to the notes to the financial statements,as these are the disclosures required by the financial reporting framework and the disclosures which have been subject to audit. Finally,the auditor's statement that 'we are confident that the parent company,Grantham Co,is able to fully support Branson Co'is not appropriate.The audit firm should not offer any opinion on the level of support being offered by the parent company. The statement could open up the audit firm to liability issues should Branson Co face further financial dificulties which thee parent company is not able to support. Contract win A KAM is one of significance to the audit,and the extract does not give any information to describe or explain why the contract was significant to the audit.If the contract was a KAM,then in accordance with ISA 701 Communicating Key Audit Matters in the Independent Auditor's Report,the auditor's report should contain a description of: - why the matter was determined to be one of the most significant in the audit and therefore determined to be a KAM,and - how the matter was addressed in the audit. Neither of these required descriptions has been provided and therefore either the contract win is not a KAM and should not be included,or it is a KAM,for example,in the auditor's evaluation of going concern,but the descriptions are inadequate and not in compliance with ISA 701. In addition,the auditor's comment regarding the contract 'eliminating going concern problems'seems to contradict both thee audit findings and the statement in the draft auditor's report which states that there are significant uncertainties over going concern.The KAM section should not be used to provide aditional explanation regarding matters disclosed or discussed elsewhere in the auditor's report,so it is not appropriate to provide additional information or an opinion about going concern in the KAM section. The auditor should not comment on the operations of the client company,such as the success in winning a tender anywhere in the auditor's report.This impairs the objectivity of the auditor's report and is not appropriate.Referring to a competitor of thee company is extremely unprofessional and could result in legal action being taken against the audit firm. In addition,/2 mark for relevant trends or calculations which form part of the evaluation of risk of material misstatement(max 2 marks).
