Agree a sample of items included in raw materials to supplier invoice and original order placed with the supplier Obtain and review the bulk purchase discount offered by the supplier and agree that specific transaction to cash book and bank statement and to goods received notes and trace into the inventory system For the work in progress: For the contracts included in work in progress,agree a sample of direct costs to supporting 一 documentation - Obtain the calculations detailing the method of overhead allocation and review for appropriateness Re-perform the work in progress calculation From a review of contract cost accounts and comparison with contract prices,discussion with management and production supervisors,determine whether any contracts could be loss making Visit the boat building facility at the year end to form an auditor's estimate of the stage of completion of each boat,and follow up in the final audit,to ensure that the work in progress of each contract appears reasonable For the finished goods: For the boat which has not passed its safety inspection,obtain the inspector's report and discuss with relevant personnel to establish the reason for the failure to obtain the licence Discuss with relevant personnel to obtain understanding of the work needed to rectify the problem and the costs involved Based on the above,determine whether the additional costs will impact on the lower of cost and net realisable value of the boat For the boat being stored at the dockyard,confirm through a review of supporting documentation,e.g. customer account and invoices raised,whether the sale of the boat has been accounted for Review the terms and conditions of the contract to determine when Encore Co completes its obligations under the contract and therefore whether the sale should be recognised and the inventory derecognised Maximum marks Marks 18 14 (c)Ethical issues 1 mark for each point discussed and 1 mark for relevant action points: - Review of internal controls should be a separate engagement with a separate fee - Service requested is an internal audit service - Not prohibited for a non-listed company,service can be provided subject to evaluation of threats to objectivity - Self-review threat explained - Separate teams to safeguard the self-review threat Assuming management responsibility-explained Matters to be acknowledged by management 一二 Loan guarantee creates a self-interest threat-explained 一 一 Loan guarantee should be declined as the loan is material to the financial statements Maximum marks Professional skills marks Communication - Briefing note format and structure-use of headings/sub-headings and an introduction - Style,language and clarity-appropriate layout and tone of briefing notes,presentation of materiality and relevant calculations,appropriate use of the CBE tools,easy to follow and understand - Effectiveness and clarity of communication-answer is relevant and tailored to the scenario - Adherence to the specific requests made by the audit engagement partner Analysis and evaluation - Effective application of technical and ethical guidance to support conclusions and recommendations - Risk evaluation is effectively prioritised,focusing on significance and only taking account of risks which would result in material misstatements - Appropriate consideration of the impact of the issues in controls in the client accounting systems has on the risks and the audit strategy Professional scepticism and professional judgement Appropriate application of professional judgement to draw conclusions and make informed decisions - following recognition of unusual or unexpected movements,missing/incomplete information or challenging presented information as part of the risk evaluation - Determination and justification of suitable materiality level appropriately and consistently applied - Appropriate use of technical knowledge and professional judgement to propose suitable audit procedures Commercial acumen - Using the information in the scenario to evaluate the magnitude and consideration of risks Maximum marks Maximum Marks 8 2 (a)Client acceptance Generally,up to 1 mark per point evaluated: ISQM1 criteria Client integrity 0 Remaining elements of customer due diligence 0 Reason for non-appointment of existing auditor 0 Risk arising from lack of existing knowledge 0 Permission to contact existing auditor 0 Potential misrepresentation of the brand value to the bank 0 Planned under disclosure to bank suggests lack of integrity 0 Manipulation risk Competence and resources re short deadline Ethical issues 0 independence considered 0 self-interest threat audit 0 self-interest threat hospitality 0 conflicts of interest 0 advocacy threat 0 safeguards Conclusion Maximum marks 12 (b) Generally,1 mark for each valid point of evaluation and/2mark for each relevant trend or calculation point discussed to a maximum of 3 marks. Examples include: 一 Sales 0 20X3 levels may not return immediately 0 Sales price inflation 0 Revenue per restaurant 0 Assumes full income from start of the period opened Supplier payments 0 Gross profit appears high 0 Assumes six months credit Lease payments 0 New leases costs Other costs 0 No cost inflation included 0 Apparently no pay rises Capital expenditure 0 No capital expenditure for existing restaurants Marketing costs 0 Appear low 0 Single period only Loan repayments omitted Loan interest on new loan omitted Tax omitted Overallconclusion Maximum marks 8 Professional skills marks Analysis and evaluation Evaluation of the available evidence by the identification of further specific sources of information in order to validate and corroborate assumptions and estimations made in the forecast by the client Professional scepticism and professional judgement Effective challenge and critical assessment of the acceptance considerations supplied with appropriate conclusions Demonstration of evaluation of the forecast and identification of the areas where professional scepticism should be applied Commercial acumen - Appropriate recognition of the wider implications on the engagement,the audit firm and the company Maximum marks Maximum 25 3 (a)(i) Deficiencies in internal control Generally,up to 1 mark for each relevant and well explained answer point: - Controls over significant operating system changes appear to be deficient - Deficiencies could lead to inaccurate tax and possible fines/penalties(implications of the control deficiencies) - Could indicate more pervasive deficiencies in the general control environment(wider implications of the control deficiencies) - Marketing director appears to have deliberately taken assets from the company - Management fraud should always be reported to those charged with governance even if not material - In both cases,recommendations for improving internal controls should be made Maximum marks (ii)Matters to be reported to Those Charged With Governance Generally,up to 1 mark for each relevant and well explained answer point: - Significant delay caused by lack of information provided to the audit team - Implications for audit efficiency and audit fee - Controls over manual journals appear to be deficient - Discuss why the same issue arose last year and has not been resolved - Communicate potential impact on financial statements of change in accounting policy regarding revaluations - Communicate practical considerations of the change in accounting policy - Ethical threat to objectivity - Management threat if partner attends board meetings - Allowed to attend but not make decisions Maximum marks (b)Key audit matters Up to 1 mark for each valid answer point which is a critique of the draft key audit matters paragraph: - Going concern matters should be in separate paragraph and are not a KAM - Description too vague and should refer to cash flow problems not losses - Should refer to the note to the financial statements,not the operating and financial review - Inappropriate to express an opinion on the level of support offered by the parent company - Contract win needs to have more explanation/discussion as to why it has been included as a KAM - Or,if not a KAM,it should not be included - Contradiction where the contract is described as eliminating going concern problems - KAM should not be used to provide additional opinion 一 Inappropriate to refer to the details of the win or to name the competitor Maximum marks Professional skills marks Analysis and evaluation Appropriate assessment of the ethical and professional issues raised,using examples where relevant to support overall comments Evaluation of the information to provide an appropriate conclusion using examples from the scenario to support the decisions made Professional scepticism and professional judgement - Effective challenge and critical assessment of the matters with appropriate conclusions - Demonstration of the ability to probe into the judgemental areas of due diligence engagements including the identification of missing information or additional information which would be required - Effective critical assessment of the evidence supplied with wider implications Commercial acumen Demonstrates commercial awareness regarding the possible professional issues which may affect the audit firm Maximum marks Maximum Marks 5 7 8 5 25 Answers Strategic Professional-Options,AAA-INT Advanced Audit and Assurance-International(AAA-INT) September/December 2023 Sample Answers 1 Briefing notes To:Paula Sanders,Group engagement partner From:Audit manager Subject:Audit planning in respect of the Hammer Group Date:1 July 20X5 Introduction These briefing notes have been prepared for your use in planning the audit of the Hammer Group for the year ending 30 September 20X5.The Group is an existing audit client,and using recent information obtained from the client,these notes evaluate thee significant business risks facing the Group.The significant risks of material misstatement are evaluated and prioritised in order to effectively plan the audit.The notes then go on to recommend the principal audit procedures to be performed in respect of the store enhancement costs planned for the year and finally discuss the ethical issues affecting our firm. (a)Evaluation of business risk Reliance on the Hammer brand The Group's success is based on the Hammer brand,and any damage to the brand could have implications for the Group maintaining its competitive advantage.The investigations into the excessive fees charged by the Hammer Bank could affect thee reputation of the brand as a whole,resulting in customers going to alternative stores,and therefore damaging market share.In addition,it is likely that the Group spends a lot on advertising to support the brand name,which will be a drain on cash flows. Competitive environment The hardware store industry is highly competitive,as ilustrated by the price war between the competitor and Mallet Co,and as indicated by the sales promotions offered by the Group.There is a risk of losing customers to competitors if these strategies are not successful.Further,aggressive pricing strategies will put pressure on profit margins and cash inflow.The current ratio has fallen from 1·4 to 1·1,which may be due to a lower cash balance. Extension and renovation programme This is a large investment for the Group,with approximately $144 million being spent on the programme(25 stores at $5·75 million each).There is a risk that there is not an adequate return on investment if the 'destination stores'strategy is not successful.In addition,with five stores being closed at any one time,there will be a significant loss of revenue and cash inflow. There may be the danger that customers,having used alternative hardware stores while the Hammer stores are shut,come to like one of their competitors and do not return once the renovated stores are reopened. In addition,there is a risk that the cost of the refurbishment project will overrun and that additional financing will be required. This would cause further deterioration in gearing and interest cover,both of which show a negative trend this year,and with implications for existing loan covenants. Acquisition of Ladder Co The acquisition of a foreign subsidiary represents a significant investment by the Group and there is a risk of the business failing and the Group losing some or all of its financial investment.There may be operational problems in terms of the Group exercising its control.The government of Farland owns 35%of the company's share capital,and it may be that the government attempts to use its shareholding and significant influence to interfere with the Group's plans for the company.Any such political interference could affect the return on investment and the Group's strategy of international expansion.This would be made worse if there is political instability or economic problems in Farland. As this is their first overseas investment,management at the Group may lack the necessary knowledge and expertise to operate a foreign subsidiary in line with local regulations. Bank loan A significant bank loan,representing 6·4%of the Group's total assets,has been taken out to partly fund the acquisition of Ladder Co.This would have contributed to the significant increase in the gearing ratio.High gearing poses a financial risk to the Group,especially due to the variable interest rate attached to the loan.If interest rates rise,the Group may struggle to meet interest payments.This is a real concern especially as interest cover shows a sharp decline during the year and gearing is high at 68%.The bank could recall the loan if the covenants are breached and the Group is unlikely to be able to repay the loan in its entirety if it is recalled. Wrench Co legal claims On a general note,the financial industry is highly regulated,and represents a diversification from the Group's other retail activities,meaning that Group management may be less experienced with this part of the Group's activities and therefore poor operational and strategic decisions may be made in relation to it. More specifically,the legal claims against Wrench Co could have significant implications.The claims themselves willput pressure on cash flow,and the cost of the claims will etrimentally affect profit.There may be many potential claims which have not yet been lodged against the Group,so the eventual cash and profit impacts may be much more than the amount of the provision which is currently recognised.The financial services regulator could take further action,for example,imposing a fine. Tutorial note: Credit will be awarded for discussion of other relevant business risks. (b)Evaluation of significant risks of material misstatement Materiality For the purposes of these briefing notes,the following overallmateriality level will be used to assess the significance of identified risks and as requested this has been based on the revenue of the company. Benchmarks 0·5%-1%of revenue =range of $2·25 million-$4·5 million This benchmark is only a starting point for determining planning materiality and professional judgement will ned to be applied in determining a final level to be applied during the course of the audit.This is an existing client and we have cumulative knowledge and experience of the Hammer Group;however,the proposed restructuring and pressures on the company profit margins in the year increases the level of risk in the current year.Therefore,materiality has been based on 0·5%of revenue at $2·25 million and has been set at $2·3 million. Exceptional items and unusual movement in margins The operating margin before exceptional items has improved from 19·4%in 20X4 to 21·6%and this is not in line with expectations.Given the Group's sales promotions and Mallet Co's price war,it would be expected that operating margins would fall as a result,and this is supported by the fact that the operating margin after exceptional items has reduced from 7·5%to 7 · 1%. There is an incentive for overstatement of operating profit before exceptional items due to the executive director's bonus.It is permissible to disclose items as exceptional items where this enhances the fair presentation of the financial statements. However,the determination of which items are to be disclosed as exceptional items is subject to judgement and users of the Group financial statements could be misled if amounts are not classified and disclosed in a transparent manner and consistently with the Group's accounting policy.In this situation,there is a particular risk of management bias due to the executive bonus being based on profit before exceptional items. It is debatable whether the store enhancement costs should be presented as exceptional as these are typically just part of normal operating expenses.It is possible that management has chosen to classify the costs as exceptional in order to maximise their bonus.Given the unusual movements,there is also a risk that profit is overstated,perhaps through deliberate accounting treatments such as accelerating revenue recognition or overvaluation of inventory. Overall,there has been a reduction in net margin from 6·2%in 20X4 to 5·3%in 20X5 and this is as expected,given the loan taken out during the year and the associated increase in finance costs. Tutorial note: Credit will be awarded for recognition that the projected financial statements may not be correctif the accounting policies used by Ladder Co have not been aligned to those of the Group for the purpose of consolidating the projected results. Capitalisation of costs in relation to the renovation and extension programme The cost of the programme is material to the Group,with the total cost being$144 million,significantly exceeding the materiality threshold calculated of $2·3 million.There is a risk that capital expenditure and revenue expenditure may be misallocated leading to over or understatement of property,plant and equipment and profit.Renovation costs would include items such as decorating,which should be expensed,while acquiring land,creating car parks and building extension work would involve construction of a new asset,and those costs should be capitalised.It is possible that management may misclassify operating costs as capital in nature to increase reported profits. Once the stores are ready for use,the assets which have been capitalised should be depreciated over their estimated useful life.With so many different types of assets being capitalised,there is a risk that their individual useful life is not appropriately determined,meaning that assets could be over or under depreciated,leading to inaccurate measurement of assets and recognition of depreciation expense within profit.It is possible that management may classify assets together to justify a longer useful life and thereby artificially inflate profits through reduced depreciation charges. Acquisition of Ladder Co-fair value of net assets acquired and goodwill Assuming that it is correct to treat Ladder Co as a subsidiary,there are significant risks of material misstatement relating to accounting for the acquisition,and the accounting treatment of goodwill,non-controling interest and the assets and liabilities acquired.According to IFRS®3 Business Combinations,all assets acquired and liabilities assumed in a business combination are measured at acquisition-date fair value,and are recognised separately from goodwill.There is a risk that not all acquired assets and assumed liabilities have been identified,and/or that they have not been measured at an appropriate fair value.Thee fair value exercise is very subjective,increasing the risk of misstatement,and the fact that Ladder Co is a foreign subsidiary may mean that the identification of assets and liabilities would be more complex.Any misstatements in the net assets recognised will impact on goodwill,meaning that non-current assets could be over or understated. Classification of Ladder Co Information is not provided regarding the value of Ladder Co's total assets,nor about the goodwill arising on acquisition,but the cost of investment in Ladder Co is material to the Group. Ladder Co has been treated as a subsidiary in the consolidated financial statements,which would appear to be the correct treatment given that the Group owns 65%of the company's share capital,which indicates control.According to IFRS 10 Consolidated Financial Statements,an investor controls an investee where the investor has power over the investee;exposure or rights to variable returns from its involvement with the investee;and the ability to use its power over the investee to affect the amount of the investor's returns.The risk is that due to the government of Farland owning 35%of Ladder Co's share capital, there may be some restriction on the Group management exercising its power,for example,if under the regulations of Farland, a shareholding of 35%is enough to block decisions,preventing the Group from truly controlling the operations of Ladder Co. In this case,consolidation as a subsidiary would not be appropriate leading to the misstatement of the consolidated financial statements as a whole. Ladder Co's use of Farland national accounting standards For the purpose of consolidation,Ladder Co's financial statements must be prepared using the same accounting policies as the rest of the Group and comply with IFRS®Accounting Standards requirements.As such,there is a need to adjust thee financial statements of Ladder Co during the consolidation process.This is an area of high inherent and control risk due to the inexperience of Group management with ensuring the consistency of accounting polices across the Group companies.There is a risk that the adjustments may not be complete,or may not be appropriate,leading to errors in the Group consolidated financial statements. Provision for legal claims against Wrench Co The provision recognised is material to the consolidated financial statements.The risk is that liabilities may be understated and profit overstated if not all claims have been recognised within the provision or if the measurement of the provision is not appropriate.The investigation into the excessive fees charged by Wrench Co is ongoing,therefore it is likely that more claims may arise.In addition,there may be a fine or penalty imposed by the financial services regulator which has not been recognised as a provision,again understating liabilities and overstating profit.There is a further risk of inadequate disclosure in the notes to the financial statements as IAS®37 Provisions,Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets requires disclosures including a brief description of the nature,timing,uncertainties and assumptions relevant to the amount recognised.An accrual may also need to be recognised in respect of legal costs associated with the claims,if this is not recognised liabilities and operating expenses willbe understated. Inventory valuation Inventory is material to the financial statements.The Group follows IAS 2 Inventories by measuring inventory at the lower of cost and net realisable value,but a risk of material misstatement arises due to the judgement involved in measuring the value of inventory due to the sales promotions.The risk is that the sales promotions which are being put in place due to the price war with the competitor,such as 'buy one get one free',could reduce the net realisable value of product lines below theircost. If effective systems are not in place to determine where this is the case and adjust inventory valuation accordingly,inventory could be overstated in value. Given the incentive for management bias discussed above,it is in management's interest to inflate inventory valuation,as this will increase operating profit and therefore the bonus to be paid. Tutorial note: Credit will also be given for the discussion of other relevant risks of material misstatement,for example,relating to the measurement of the non-controlling interest in Ladder Co,and the risk associated with opening balances in Ladder Co not having been audited by Stanley Associates. Conclusion The Group audit is considered to be relatively high risk due to the numerous business risks and risks of material misstatements which have been identified.The risks have been prioritised in respect of the magnitude and likelihood of the issue. The acquisition and classification of Ladder Co should be considered the most significant risk during the audit,due to thee nature of it being acquired during the year,the potential for management bias in the classification of it as a subsidiary with thee Group,in addition to the treatment of goodwill.Business risks identified from the acquisition due to itbeing the first foreign subsidiary for the Group,the subsidiary not adopting IFRS Accounting Standards and the potential influence over the decisions of the company by the Farland government make this an area where aditional audit work and even the use of local experts will be required. In addition to the risks of the Ladder Co acquisition,the restructuring project will need careful audit scrutiny to ensure thee completeness and accuracy of the accounting treatment,in particular,which costs have been capitalised. Finally,the significance of the risk of management bias in the presentation of the expenses in the statement of profit or loss requires the audit team to effectively challenge the proposed accounting presentation in the Group financial statements. Therefore,the audit strategy and programme must be carefully developed to reduce the overall audit risk to an acceptable level whilst focusing on these significant issues. (c)Principal audit procedures in respect of the expenditure on the store enhancement programme 一 To develop an understanding regarding the programme,obtain relevant supporting documentation,including a capital expenditure forecast. Discuss with management to form an understanding on the progress made on the programme by the year end,i.e.how many of the 25 stores are now fully renovated,how many are closed for renovation,the number of stores on which renovation work has not yet commenced. - Compare the costs incurred to date to an approved capital expenditure budget for the programme,and discuss significant variances with an appropriate employee,for example,the project manager. - Arrange to physically inspect several stores to confirm the existence of their renovation at the year end. - For any stores being renovated at the year end,obtain a stage of completion certificate from the building contractor to allow an assessment of the costs capitalised. Perform substantive analytical procedures by comparing the amounts capitalised in relation to each store and discuss any unexplained anomalies with management to identify whether appropriate amounts have been capitalised and meet the criteria to be considered an asset rather than an expense. - Obtain a breakdown of the total costs incurred by the year end and for a sample of expenditure: - Agree to supporting documentation,e.g.site acquisition costs to legal documentation,fixtures such as shelving to suppliers'invoices. - Evaluate the nature of the expenditure,i.e.whether appropriately accounted for as capital or revenue in nature. - For appropriately capitalised costs,agree the amount is included in the non-current asset register. For the labour costs,enquire with management to understand the basis of the amount capitalised and agree a sample of costs,e.g.wages per hour,to payroll records and timesheets. Test the operating effectiveness of management's internal controls over the classification of costs between capital and revenue expenditure by reviewing a sample of invoices from both classifications and agreeing the nature of the costs. Review any relevant signed contracts,e.g.from building contractors,electricians,architects,etc,and discuss any significant deviations from the amounts stated in the contract and the amount capitalised. (d)Ethical issues The Group has asked Stanley Associates for advice on its international expansion and also to perform adjustments to Ladder Co's financial statements to align its accounting policies with IFRS Accounting Standards and Group accounting policies.This raises several ethical issues for the firm. First,providing advice on the selection of target companies and on the financing of investments is the provision of non-audit services,specifically corporate finance services,to an audited entity.The IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (the Code)states that the provision of non-audit services is likely to give rise to threats to objectivity and independence,in particular familiarity,self-interest,self-review and advocacy threats are possible. According to the Code,factors which are relevant in evaluating the level of such threats created by providing corporate finance services to an audit client include: - The degree of subjectivity involved in determining the appropriate treatment for the outcome or consequences of the corporate finance advice in the financial statements. 一 The extent to which: - The outcome of the corporate finance advice will directly affect amounts recorded in the financial statements. - The amounts are material to the financial statements. - Whether the effectiveness of the corporate finance advice depends on a particular accounting treatment or presentation in the financial statements. In providing advice on the selection of target companies and on financing arrangements,the main risk is that the audit firm could be seen to be taking on the responsibility of management.Assuming a management responsibility creates a familiarity threat because the firm becomes too closely aligned with the views and interests of management.The Code states that an audit firm shall not assume a management responsibility for an audit client. Providing the advice also creates a sel-interest threat due to the fees which will be payable for the advice provided,particularly due to the size of the fee which could influence Stanley Associates when performing this service.The fact that the fee for this service will amount to 75%of the audit fee indicates that the fee will be significant and in combination with the audit fee and fees for any other services provided to the Group,an actual or perceived fee dependency could be created meaning that the firm will be motivated to act in ways which ensure retention of the client. A self-review threat arises in that when auditing the Group,the audit firm would be evaluating risk and performing audit procedures pertaining to the advice which they had given and would be less likely to highlight issues or challenge assumptions. This impacts on the quality of the audit performed. Advice on finance in particular is likely to also lead to an advocacy threat,where the audit firm is seen to be promoting thee interests of the audit client,for example,to a potential provider of finance and that the firm is promoting or confirming thee company's ability to fulfil the debt obligations. The audit firm should consider whether safeguards could be used to reduce the threats to an acceptable level,for example: - A written acknowledgement from management that they are responsible for decision-making regarding the planned international expansion and associated fnance; - The use of separate teams of staff to provide the advice and to work on the audit where the provider of the other services should avoid expressing any opinion on the approach and conclusions which will be made by the audit team when considering accounting and other judgements; - Review and monitoring of the situation by an independent audit partner;and - The use of an engagement quality review to provide effective challenge to both the audit engagement partner and the partner responsible for performance of the other services who is aware of the circumstances leading to significant self-review threats. If such safeguards are considered to reduce the threats to an acceptable level,then the advice could be provided.The matter should be discussed with the Group's audit committe,which should approve the provision of these non-audit services by thee audit firm. Without further detailon the nature of the work which will be required,it is not possible to assess whether the safeguards available would be sufficient in this case. Adjustments to Ladder Co's financial statements In relation to performing the adjustments to Ladder Co's financial statements,the Code states that providing accounting and bookkeeping services to an audit client might create a self-review threat.This threat arises because should the audit firm perform the necessary adjustments which form part of the consolidation process,the audit team is likely to over-rely on thee work of their colleagues and not rigorously audit the adjustments. The Code states that unless the services are of a routine or mechanical nature,they should not be provided.Given thee complexity of adjusting financial statements from national accounting standards to IFRS Accounting Standards,this is not a routine or mechanical task,and the service should not be provided. Conclusion It is recommended that our firm does not offer advice to the Group on its planned expansion due to the significance of ethical threats to independence and objectivity which would arise. 2 (a)(i) Evaluate the matters in respect of quality management which should be considered by Aster &Co before accepting the engagement When determining whether to enter into a professional relationship with a new client,a firm is required to assess whether this would be possible under the criteria of ISQM 1 Quality Management for Firms that Perform Audits or Reviews of Financial Statements or other Assurance or Related Service Engagements. This means Aster &Co shall make professional judgements based on the following quality objectives regarding whether to accept the engagement: - Information obtained about the nature and circumstances of the engagement; - Consideration of the integrity and ethical values of the client;and - The firm's ability to perform the engagement in accordance with professional standards and applicable legal and regulatory requirements. Nature and extent of the engagement The nature of the engagement is an agreed upon procedures engagement to quantify a loss from fraud for the purposes of an insurance claim. ISQM 1 provides guidance on the information to be obtained with respect to understanding the nature of the entity.This will include obtaining information on the operations of Baker Co,their organisation structure,the business model and how the company is financed. Given the company is owned by a foreign trust,Aster &Co will be required to ascertain information about the trustees and beneficiaries of the trust when assessing ownership,organisation structure and financing.This is likely to include reviewing the trust deed and the purpose and location of the trust,an enhanced risk assessment,obtaining details of any donors to the trust and their domicile and enhanced money laundering checks. ISRS 4400(Revised)Agreed-upon Procedures Engagements also specifically states that before accepting an agreed upon procedures engagement,the firm should ensure that the procedures are appropriate for the purposes of the engagement. Integrity and ethical values of the client Aster &Co willbe required to perform customer due diligence and,in particular,will be expected to consider the identity and business reputation of Baker Co's principal owners,key management,and those charged with governance.This will also involve obtaining documents to prove the identity of such people using a passport or other legal documents and considering whether there are any indications that the client might be involved in money laundering or other criminal activities. Baker Co has a good business reputation and their record of charitable donations gives some confidence in the ethical values of the client. The foreign trust and complex ownership structure should be understood in terms of motivation and history of the arrangement.Sometimes structures such as this are associated with money laundering,where the funds placed in the system are layered through complex transactions and business structures or may be created for purposes of tax avoidance. As such,Aster &Co will need to obtain a detailed understanding of why the ownership structure was formed this way and any reluctance to be transparent over such issues would make it less likely Aster &Co would accept the client. It should be considered why Baker Co has approached Aster &Co,rather than their existing auditors.This may be because they do not have the appropriate specialisms or are prevented through the terms of the insurance cover when appointing an independent firm to quantify the loss arising as a result of the fraud. Further information should be obtained about the client through contacting the client auditors and discussions with other third parties such as bankers,legal counsel and industry peers.Permission would need to be obtained from Baker Co prior to such contact.If Baker Co were to refuse permission,then Aster &Co should not accept them as a client.Background searches of local regulatory databases should also be performed,for example,in the United Kingdom,a Companies' House search should be performed. There is no evidence to suggest an aggressive fee reduction or client-imposed limitation of scope which would hinder Aster &Co's acceptance or potential threat to the objectivity of the assurance team. Performance of the engagement in accordance with professional,legal and regulatory requirements It would be expected that as a firm operating with a forensic services department that the staff within the firm would have appropriate competence and capabilities to perform the engagement. The availability of the resources to perform the engagement in such a short period of time should be assessed as it is possible that Aster &Co does not have sufficient staff to be allocated to this engagement at this time. Aster &Co should also assess whether there is a need to deploy specific IT resources to perform the assignment which are compatible with Baker Co's existing and new management information systems. Given that Baker Co is not curently a client of the firm,the firm's independence will not have been previously considered. In this regard,it is important to ensure there are no threats to the firm's objectivity which might prevent it from accepting the appointment.If such independence threats exist,this would undermine the integrity of the report given that the insurance company requires an independent quantification of the loss. Aster &Co should also consider whether there are any conflicts of interest between Baker Co and any of the firm's existing clients.Where any such conflicts exist,Aster &Co would be required to notify both Baker Co and the existing client explaining the situation and proposed safeguards which will be implemented to mitigate the risk.Permission in writing will be required from both parties in order to proceed with the engagement. Tutorial note: Candidates are not expected to have knowledge of operation of trusts but to recognise from the definition provided that there are additional considerations regarding the ownership structure of the company,and how this may influence the acceptance considerations of Aster &Co. (ii)Explain the specific aspects of the terms of engagement which should be agreed Identification of the subject matter Aster &Co needs to agree the detailed scope of the engagement,in particular,how many years should be subject to procedures and whether the investigation will cover both the existing and new management information system data or simply the existing system which is being replaced. Purpose of the engagement and intended users of the report Aster &Co will need to agree with Baker Co and their insurer the specific purpose of the engagement,i.e.to quantify the loss arising as a result of this payroll fraud.It should be made clear that this will not identify other types of fraud for which procedures have not been included in the engagement. Aster &Co will need confirmation that Baker Co will grant them permission to communicate with third parties such as the police and the company's insurance company regarding the investigations. It should also be agreed who can make use of the report.It is possible that the users are limited to Baker Co and their insurers but it may be used to pursue a claim against any fraudster or in a police investigation.Aster &Co is likely to restrict their liability to specific agreed users of the report. Responsible party Aster &Co will agree a responsible party with Baker Co who will be responsible for the subject matter on which the assurance engagement willbe performed.In this instance,it is likely the finance director will assume this role. Nature of the engagement The engagement terms will state that the engagement is to provide factual results of the agreed upon procedures. This is not an assurance engagement and Aster &Co will not express an opinion or provide an assurance conclusion. The agreed-upon procedures will be determined between both Baker Co and Aster &Co to ensure that they are appropriate for the purpose of the engagement. Identification of the addressee of the report Aster &Co will establish whether the report should be addressed to Baker Co or to the insurance company. Nature,timing and extent of procedures The procedures to be performed should be described in clear terms ensure that management understands what Aster &Co will and will not be performing. Reference to the expected form and content of the report Aster &Co should agree with Baker Co the format of the report they will issue and what those contents will be.In this respect,Aster &Co will need to understand any requirements which the insurance company has in this regard. (b )Recommend the procedures to be performed to quantify the loss for the purpose of the insurance claim Obtain a list of staff who are authorised to amend payroll details and determine who is able to alter bank details and who has the responsibility for removing leavers from payroll. - Use of data analytics to analyse the payroll records for the specified period of time to determine any alteration of payroll details such as bank accounts and analyse whether appropriate authorisation was made for each change. Use of data analytics to analyse the payroll records to determine: - Any individual on the payroll who has no contact details. - Any bank account receiving the pay of more than one individual as the fraud identified involved the redirection of payments into a single bank account. For those potentially suspicious bank accounts,determine through payrollrecords whether there is a genuine reason (e.g.joint bank account of married employees)to eliminate those accounts from the investigation. Reconciliation of employees in the payrolldatabase with employees in the human resources database to identify any ghost employees where the member of staff has left the company but continues to be paid via payroll. Use data analytics to identify the period and amounts which were paid to the names identified as ghost employees and to quantify all payments made into the bank accounts identified as suspicious. Identify all payments made to employees who are not genuine employees and calculate the total amount for assessing the quantification of the fraud. Attempt to identify the beneficiaries of the bank accounts to which funds have been diverted through payroll records if it relates to a bank account belonging to an existing employee or through discussion with the police and bank if possible. 3 (a)Matters to discuss with management and recommended further actions (i) Investment properties The uncorrected misstatement is below the material level determined for Sunningdale Co's financial statements at $25,000 and therefore immaterial. It is incorrect for management not to recognise the loss on revaluation of one of its investment properties on the change to a fair value model when it is recognising gains on the three other investment properties. IAS 40 Investment Properties states that an entity can choose either the cost model or the fair value model.The fair value model requires that investment properties are recognised in the statement of financial position at fair value.Gains and losses on revaluation when using the fair value model are recognised in the statement of profit or loss.One method must be adopted for all of an entity's investment properties;they must all be accounted for using the same basis and change is only permitted if this results in a more appropriate presentation. It is likely that moving to a fair value model will result in a more appropriate presentation,indeed IAS 40 notes that it is highly unlikely for a change from a fair value model to a cost model.The fact that three investment properties will be revalued at a fair value which is higher than the current carrying amount,and a gain recognised in the financial statements demonstrates that management may be adopting the fair value model in order to increase the company's assets and profit.If the necessary adjustments are not made by management,the investment property which has not been revalued will cause the assets and profit to be overstated. The selective,and incorrect,use of a change in valuation model could be interpreted as an indication of a lack of management integrity which could extend to other areas of the accounts. (ii)Specialist manufacturing equipment Matters to discuss with management The uncorrected misstatement is above the materiality level determined for Sunningdale Co's financial statements at $140,000 and therefore material. It is factually incorrect for management to recognise the change in useful life as a retrospective adjustment,and furthermore to enter the total of this amendment through the current year's profit or loss statement.The change in useful life is a change in accounting estimate and the reason for the change has not been adequately justified by management. The residual value and useful life of an asset should be reviewed at least at each financial year end and,if expectations differ from previous estimates,any change is accounted for prospectively as a change in estimate under IAS 8 Accounting Policies,Changes in Accounting Estimates and Errors. Under IAS 16 Property,Plant and Equipment,for all depreciable assets an entity should allocate depreciation on a systematic basis over the asset's useful life and the depreciation method used should reflect the pattern in which the asset's economic benefits are consumed by the entity.Changes in accounting estimates result from new information or new developments,however,management has not explained the basis of the new information that necessitates a change in the estimate of the useful life.Further investigation should be carried out to provide evidence to support or dispute management's assertion that an additional five years better reflects the pattern of economic benefits. The lack of a justifiable reason for the change of depreciation method,coupled with the material misstatement to profit would cause the auditors to question the integrity of management for proposing these changes. (iii)Gift of cars The transactions are material by nature because it is a transaction between the company and two close relatives of a member of key management personnel,meaning that it is a related party transaction,disclosure of which is required in accordance with IAS 24 Related Party Disclosures.It is irrelevant that the son and daughter of the director have not been employed by Sunningdale Co as they are related parties due to their close family relationship with a member of key management personnel. ISA 550 Related Parties requires that the auditor evaluates whether identified related party relationships and transactions have been appropriately accounted for and disclosed in accordance with the applicable financial reporting framework. The auditor should also obtain audit evidence that the transactions have been appropriately authorised and approved. Tutorial note: Credit will be awarded for further discussion of relevant points from ISA 550 including consideration of whether the transaction is in the normal course of business and whether the terms of the transaction appear consistent with management's explanations. IAS 24 requires full disclosure of the following in respect of related party transactions,including: - the nature of the related party relationship, - the amount of the transaction, - the amount of any balances outstanding including terms and conditions. The fact that no income was received for the motor vehicles does not remove the requirement for the transactions to be fully disclosed and may indicate that this is a form of disguised remuneration for the director in an attempt to avoid tax. Further actions - Management should be requested to correct the misstatements in respect of the investment properties and the specialist manufacturing equipment. Cottrell &Co should consider the fact that other audit areas could have also been manipulated to achieve required targets/covenants and may need to plan more audit work accordingly. Cottrell &Co should consider the independence of the valuer,especially in the light that three investment properties have increased in value,but one has decreased-it is possible that the increases have been overstated. If management refuses to adjust the financial statements,the auditor should communicate again with those charged with governance.They should repeat the request to adjust the financial statements and inform them of the modifications which would be made to the auditor's opinion if the adjustments are not made. If management stillrefuses to amend the financial statements,the auditor should request a written representation from management confirming their intent to proceed without amending the financial statements and that they understand that the audit opinion will be modified in this event. A second partner review of the matter and the proposed auditor's report to be issued should be obtained. (b)Implications for the auditor's report When considered in aggregate,the incorrect retrospective accounting treatment of the accumulated depreciation of thee specialist machinery and the lack of disclosure of related party transactions are material misstatements due to non-compliance of requirements set out in IFRS Accounting Standards. The auditor willneed to apply the requirements of ISA 705 Modifications to the Opinion in the Independent Auditor's Report. In this case,it is likely that the auditor would conclude that the misstatements in aggregate are material but not pervasive to the financial statements.For the misstatements to be considered pervasive,the auditor's judgement would lead to a conclusion that they represent a substantial proportion of the financial statements or in relation to disclosures,that they are fundamental to users'understanding of the financial statements,neither of which appears to be the case in this situation. Therefore,a qualified opinion on the basis of material misstatement should be given.The auditor's report should include a heading 'Qualified Opinion',and the report should state that except for the matters described in the Basis for Qualified Opinion paragraph,the financial statements give a true and fair view. The Basis for Qualified Opinion paragraph,which should be placed beneath the Qualified Opinion,should describe and quantify the material misstatement relating to the retrospective accounting treatment of the change in accounting estimate and explain how the related party disclosures are misstated.
