Appropriate application of the materiality set for the audit Commercial acumen - Appropriate recognition of the wider implications on the engagement,the audit firm and the company Maximum marks Maximum 5 11 5巧4 5 巧 Answers Strategic Professional-Options,AAA-INT Advanced Audit and Assurance-International (AAA-INT) September/December 2024 Sample Answers 1 Briefing notes To:Tim Webber,Group audit engagement partner From:Audit manager Subject:Audit planning in relation to the Hart Group Date:1 July 20X5 Introduction These briefing notes have been prepared to assist in planning the audit of the Hart Group(the Group)for the year ending 30 September 20X5.The notes begin with an evaluation of the significant business risks facing the Group followed by an evaluation of the significant risks of material misstatement which should be considered in planning the Group audit.The significant risks of material misstatement have been structured to prioritise the risks in terms of the likelihood and magnitude of misstatement in relation to each risk.The notes then focus on the significant risks of material misstatement arising on acquisition of the Group's new subsidiary,Morton Co,and includes relevant audit procedures.Finall,the notes discuss the responsibility of the auditor in relation to the Group's non-compliance with laws and regulations. (a)Evaluation of significant business risks First time acquisition Hart Co,the parent company,made its first acquisition during the year.There is always a risk with acquisitions that thee expected return on investment does not materialise due to over optimistic assumptions or failure to integrate the new business. There is also a risk that management attention is too focused on the newly acquired theatres and there is a deterioration in controls or performance within the existing business.This risk is exacerbated by the fact that the transaction was completed quickly,and no final due diligence report on Morton Co was prepared prior to acquisition,resulting in a significant risk that thee Group becomes exposed to unknown or unexpected issues or liabilities associated with the newly acquired theatres. Breach of funding covenants and withdrawal of finance The Group is financed by venture capitalists with a significant amount of debt financing.This finance is conditional on meeting covenants,specifically that operating profit margin does not fall below 6%. The Group's operating profit margin has fallen from 7·1%in 20X4 to a projected 6·3%in 20X5 which is very close to thee 6%operating profit margin requirement.In addition,the Group is loss making.As such,a small increase in costs of only $1 ·24 million could result in the breach of this covenant. Were this to happen,the Group would have to focus on rectifying this within a two-year period or financing willbe withdrawn. This may mean that the Group is required to focus on short-term actions at the expense of long-term strategy.In addition,were the finance to be withdrawn,it is likely that the Group might have to sell a substantial amount of its assets to meet the resulting obligation. Laws and regulations The Group is subject to many laws and regulations but there are particular concerns surrounding the Group's compliance with historical buildings regulations after works were performed without proper permission being obtained.While it appears as if part of the modification has been made to ensure that the theatre is accessible to all,these changes may not have been done in line with the necessary requirements and therefore the regulatory authority may still not accept this as a valid reason for carrying out the changes without permission.The regulatory authority in the past has taken legal action where rules have not been followed,and therefore,if the modifications have been carried out without due consideration to the historical features of the buildings,this could have serious consequences for the Group.Breaches in laws and regulations could lead to large fines, legal actions,and the risk of theatre closures with a significant detrimental impact on cash flow and profitability. Failures to attract quality productions The success of the Group is highly dependent on being able to attract quality productions to perform at their theatres.Failure to attract productions which give rise to strong ticket sales will reduce income from venue hire,ticket sales commissions and any other services offered to the production company such as marketing.Should any of the theatres operate at low capacity or if there is a significant amount of time where the theatres are not hosting performances,then it will be dificult for the Group to cover its fixed costs.The quality and popularity of the production potentially also has an impact on the liabilities of the Group as discussed below in terms of the ticket guarantees. Guarantees The Group appears to provide minimum ticket income guarantees to theatre production companies using their theatres.If a production fails to achieve these minimum amounts due to poor ticket sales,as has happened with the production running at the Group's Phantom Theatre,the Group will be required to make payments under these contracts impacting both cash flow and profits.This gives rise to volatility in revenue and cash flows which may be hard to predict.These amounts can be large as can be seen from the expected $4-5 million payment highlighted in relation to the Phantom Theatre.If this is replicated across multiple performances and venues,the cash impact may be extremely significant. Government grants The Group receives grants for different purposes.Some of these grants contribute to additional services which are offered suchh as the educational grant.Other grants received contribute to the maintenance and preservation of the theatre buildings.There is a risk the Group fails to obtain grant funding in the future.While the educational services are presumably discretionary costs, the preservation and maintenance of the buildings is required for the theatres to function.If such grants are not obtained,thenn the Group may struggle to fund the works,putting strain on cash flow or risking theatre closures if the buildings become unsafe due to lack of maintenance.As much of the amounts spent in this area are likely to be revenue costs rather than capital,this could have a serious impact on profits.The Group is already in a loss-making position,and this could increase significantly were the Group unable to obtain support to maintain the theatres. Tutorial note: Credit will also be awarded for additional valid business risks such as the risk that customers may be lost due to the state of the buildings or that buildings become unfit for use. (b)Evaluation and prioritisation of significant risks of material misstatement Materiality For the purposes of these briefing notes,the following overall materiality level will be used to assess the significance of identified risks and as requested this has been based on the company's revenue. Benchmark based on revenue as per Group engagement partner's instruction: 1-2%of revenue*=range of $4·46 million-$8·92 million. (*Examining team also accepted 0·5%-1%of revenue) This benchmark is only a starting point for determining materiality and professional judgement will need to be exercised in determining a final level to be applied during the course of the audit.This is an existing client,and we have cumulative knowledge and experience of the parent company.However,given that this will be the first year consolidated financial statements will be produced and the acquisition of Morton Co have increased the level of risk significantly in the current year. Therefore,materiality for the purposes of these briefing notes has been determined at the lower end of the range and set at $4·5 million. Failure to meet debt covenants and possible management bias The Group is subject to both revenue growth and operating profit margin covenants in order to retain its current financing.Thee Group is projecting agrowth in revenue for the year of 21-5%.Some of this growth is likely to be due to the acquisition of three additional theatres,however,the number of theatres has only increased by 6·4%.The rate of growth in revenue is therefore high in relation to the increased scale of operations and may indicate an overstatement of revenue. Operating profit has grown by 7·7%which is much lower than the rate of growth in revenue,hence the growth in revenue has not resulted in a corresponding profit growth.It is possible management may have recognised inappropriate amounts in revenue to attain optimistic growth targets.This could be achieved through the early recognition of more judgemental areas of revenue such as marketing revenue. The operating profit margin has fallen from 7·1%to 6·3%which is very close to the 6%target in the Group's covenants. An increase in costs of $1 ·24 million would bring operating profit margins below the target in the covenants,increasing thee risk that management may manipulate the financial statements so that costs are understated or other income from grants is overstated in order to maintain the margin. If the Group is in breach of any of its covenants and as such is at risk of funding being withdrawn,then disclosures relating to the uncertainty of the going concern status of the Group would be required.If adequate disclosures were not made by thee Group,then this would give rise to a qualitative material misstatement. The dependency on the debt finance for continued operation of the Group and the requirement to achieve covenants to maintain funding create pressures on management to exhibit bias in their selection of policies and judgements relating to estimates which enable covenants to be met. Government grants The Group has received a$15 million educational grant during the year which is above the calculated threshold and therefore is a material amount. IAS®20 Accounting for Government Grants and Disclosure of Government Assistance requires that government grants are recognised in profit or loss on a systematic basis over the periods in which the entity recognises expenses for the related costs for which the grants are intended to compensate.In the case of grants related to assets,this requires recognition of the grant as deferred income or deducting it from the carrying amount of the asset. This is a revenue grant obtained to provide services over a three-year period and should be recognised over that period in line with when the related costs are incurred.In the circumstances,this would usually be when the involvement with the local schools commences and in line with the conditions of the grant based on either the hours spent,or the number of children involved in the programme as a proportion of the total numbers to participate over the duration of the programme.As none of this has taken place yet due to staff llness,none of the grant income should be recognised to date,however,the Group has recognised $2·5 million during the year.This results in an understatement of the loss before tax and understatement of deferred income.If this has been incorrectly included in operating profit,then the Group has breached its covenants with the finance providers.As such,this is highly material in nature due to its contribution to reducing the loss for the year. There is a risk the Group may be in breach of the grant terms having not yet provided any of the educational services.If this is the case,then the grant may become repayable,and a provision would be required.As the grant should currently be recognised as deferred income,then total liabilities would not be affected.However,there is a risk that the classification of liabilities may be incorrect which may be seriously misleading for the users of the financial statements. Upgrade and maintenance costs The Group incurs high costs in relation to upgrade and maintenance of its theatres.Costs of $20 million,which are material, have been incured so far during the year to 30 September 20X5.There is a risk that costs are not appropriately distinguished between capital expenditure and operating expenditure.Upgrade costs,such as the replacement seating,should be capitalised, but maintenance costs should be expensed.There is a risk that assets are overstated,and expenses understated,if operating expenses have been inappropriately capitalised.A further risk relates to depreciation expenses,which will be overstated if capital expenditure is overstated. Tutorial note: Credit will aso be awarded for discussion of component depreciation and diferent useful lives relating to the upgrades in the theatres. Ticket sale guarantee at Phantom Theatre The ticket sale guarantee with respect to the current theatre production at the Phantom Theatre willhave created a contingent liability of $18 million.Based on present estimates,a payment of $4·5 million will be required with respect to this guarantee and therefore is material to the financial statements. This figure has been estimated by the finance director and assumes an increased level of sales in the six months to 31 December 20X5.This estimate may be subject to significant uncertainty and is a potential area where the finance director has made overly optimistic judgements in order to avoid a breach of operating profit covenants. The $4-5 million appears to satisfy the criteria for a provision and as such,a liability of $4·5 million should be created as a provision.Should the Group fail to provide for this expected payment under the guarantee,then the loss for the year would be understated and the liabilities understated as a result. Tutorial note: Credit will also be available for additional valid risks of material misstatement such as identifying that theatres in poor repair will triger an impairment review on non-current assets under IAS 36,potentially missing provisions for the fines/unauthorised modifications to the theatres in line with IAS 37. (c)Acquisition of Morton Co Morton Co was acquired on the first day of the financial year and will have been consolidated for a full year.There are a number of significant risks arising from the acquisition of Morton Co which relate to the non-routine and judgemental impacts on the financial statements such as the fair value of assets and the value of goodwill.The goodwillarising on acquisition of $14·8 million is significant and material to the financial statements.The risks arising on the acquisition are discussed in more detail below: First time consolidation Hart Co has made its first acquisition during the year which will require consolidated financial statements to be produced for the first time.Management is likely to be inexperienced in the preparation of consolidated financial statements and thee calculation of goodwill which may result in significant errors in various components of the consolidated financial statements. In addition,the Group may not have systems in place to appropriately identify allintra group transactions which will increase overall control risk within the Group. Cost of acquisition The cost of acquisition included in the goodwill calculation includes deferred consideration which is payable in 20X6;this has been measured at present value in accordance with the requirements of IFRS®3 Business Combinations.However,there is a risk the discount factor applied to the amount payable is not based on an appropriate interest rate,meaning the cost of acquisition and liability recognised in respect of the deferred consideration could be over or understated. In the Group financial statements,the goodwill figure and liability in relation to the deferred consideration are at risk of misstatement.The deferred consideration is contingent on the Morton Co theatres attaining certain revenue targets.Under IFRS 3,contingent consideration must be measured at fair value at the time of the business combination and is considered in the determination of goodwill.The payment due will be recognised as a liability.If the amount of contingent consideration changes as a resultof a post-acquisition event(such as meeting an earnings target),this liability should be adjusted to fair value and the difference expensed to the statement of profit or loss for the year.Two areas of risk arise in this regard,first thee initial recognition of this contingent consideration has been prepared based on the full amount being payable.There is an uncertainty over the achievement of those revenue targets which would mean the fair value is lower than $10 million resulting in an overstatement of goodwill and an overstatement of liabilities.There is also a risk that performance in the current year since acquisition has been lower than originally expected,and the targets may not be met,hence the fair value of this liability should be reduced which would result in the overstatement of liabilities and the understatement of profit. Identifiable net assets IFRS 3 requires that all identifiable assets and liabilities acquired are measured at fair value on the acquisition date separate from goodwill n relation to the value of goodwill there is a risk that the fair value adjustments which have been made to thee assets at the date of acquisition are incomplete or not measured at an appropriate fair value.The adjustment of $4 million which has been made in respect of the customer database is significant and very close to the materiality threshold in its own right.As a non-routine transaction which is subject to a high risk of bias,this gives rise to a risk as assets could be over or understated which would have a subsequent effect on the amortisation charged on these assets. The fact that the Morton Co brand has not been recognised as a separate asset also gives rise to a risk of misstatement.In line with IFRS 3,acquired brand names should be recognised even if they have not been recognised in the individual financial statements of the acquired company.This is because there is always sufficient information to reliably measure the fair value of these assets.There is no 'reliable measurement exception for such assets.There is therefore a risk the Morton Co brand name will not be recognised in the Group financial statements,in breach of the requirements of IFRS 3. The risks discussed above are increased by the fact that the fair values were determined by the management of Morton Co, who may not have the competence or experience to estimate fair values in accordance with IFRS 13 Fair Value Measurement. There is also a risk the management of Morton Co would be biased in their valuation of these assets in order to attract a higher price from the Group for the acquisition. Impairment of goodwill and other intangible assets IAS 36 /mpairment of Assets requires that goodwill and intangible assets with an indefinite life are tested for impairment at the end of the reporting period and other non-current assets are tested for impairment where there is an indicator of impairment such as a fall in demand. An asset is impaired where the recoverable amount of the asset is lower than the carrying amount,where the recoverable amount is the higher of an asset's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use.The goodwill will therefore need to be tested for impairment. It is likely the customer database willhave an indefinite useful ife,however,management should consider whether there are any indicators of impairment in relation to the customer database which would trigger an impairment review.Such an indicator may be the loss of customers from the list or lower than expected sales to those customers. If any of the assets are impaired,there is a risk the asset is overstated,and expenses understated if an impairment review is not performed,or the impairment loss recorded is insufficient.As this involves significant judgements and estimates of future events,there is a high level of audit risk associated with this area. Procedures-goodwill arising on acquisition of Morton Co Obtain the legal documentation pertaining to the acquisition of Morton Co and review to confirm: 0 the figures included in the goodwill calculation relating to consideration paid and payable are accurate and complete, 0 the timing and any conditions relating to the$10 million deferred payment in September 20X6, 0 the Group has obtained a 100%shareholding and that this conveys control. Agree the $22 million cash paid to the bank statement and cash book. Review the board minutes for discussions relating to the acquisition and for the relevant minute of board approval and Morton Co revenue targets. For the deferred contingent consideration,obtain management's calculation of the present value of $8·3 million,and evaluate assumptions used in the calculation with respect to the discount rate applied. Obtain details of actual revenue for the year ending 30 September 20X5 and projected revenue for the year ending 30 September 20X6 for Morton Co and assess the likelihood of the revenue targets being met by 30 September 20X6 to assess the appropriateness of recognising the fair value of the contingent consideration as certain. Obtain a statement of financial position at 1 October 20X4 for Morton Co and agree to management accounts to obtain the carrying amount of the assets and liabilities of Morton Co prior to fair value adjustments. Obtain a copy of the calculations prepared by Morton Co's management with respect to the valuations of the customer list and 0 cast the calculation to ensure its accuracy;and 0 agree the fair value adjustments to the goodwill calculation to ensure figures have been correctly reflected. Evaluate the methods used to determine the fair value of the customer list,including the assumptions involved and assess whether they are consistent with the auditor's understanding. Obtain an expert valuation of the customer list and compare with that of management to ascertain the appropriateness of management's calculation. Assess the completeness of fair value adjustments to Morton Co's assets and liabilities through: 0 discussion with the management of Morton Co to identify any unrecorded liabilities or indicators of impairment of assets, 0 review of the statement of financial position of Morton Co to identify any non-current assets where market value may exceed carrying amount, 0 enquiries of Morton Co's legal advisers regarding any litigation the company may be facing. Review the calculation of net assets acquired to confirm that Group accounting policies have been applied in determining the value of assets and liabilities acquired. Conclusion In conclusion,there are several significant risks of material misstatement.Some are at the financial statement level,for example,the possible lack of experience of the management team in relation to consolidated financial statements,and some at the assertion level,for example,the measurement of goodwill on the acquisition of Morton Co and the treatment of thee government grant received in the year.Specific areas at risk of bias arise in the calculation of goodwill and also in revenue and operating costs due to the specific financing covenants the Group is required to comply with.Our audit plan will need to be robust with respect to all the matters described in this section of the briefing notes whilst focusing on the significant areas of measurement of goodwill and the first consolidation of financial statements completed by this management team. (d )Auditor's responsibilities in relation to the Group's compliance with laws and regulations The Group appears to be in breach of relevant laws and regulations regarding building works at historical sites.ISA 250 Consideration of Laws and Regulations in an Audit of Financial Statements states that while it is management's responsibility to ensure the entity's operations are conducted in accordance with the provisions of laws and regulations,the auditor does have some responsibility in relation to compliance with laws and regulations,especially where a non-compliance has a direct impact on the financial statements. The auditor is required by ISA 315(Revised)Identifying and Assessing the Risks of Material Misstatement to gain an understanding of the legal and regulatory framework in which the audited entity operates.This will help the auditor to identify non-compliance and to assess the implications of non-compliance.Therefore,Broadway &Co should ensure they have a full knowledge and understanding of the relevant building regulations in order to evaluate the implications of non-compliance by the Group. ISA 250 requires when non-compliance is identified or suspected,the auditor shall obtain an understanding of the nature of the act and the circumstances in which it has occurred,and further information to evaluate the possible effect on the financial statements.Therefore,procedures will have to be performed to obtain evidence about the instances of non-compliance in relation to buildings work and maintenance.For example,to discuss the breach with management to understand how building work was able to commence before permission was obtained,whether this was due to deliberate action or unintentional mistake,and who was responsible. In addition,the audit team should perform further procedures,for example,discussion with the Group's legal advisers to understand the legal consequences of the breach in relation to works undertaken without the relevant permission. In relation to the unauthorised buildings work,fines and penalties may be incurred by the Group and this may be material in value as a result of the failure to obtain permission.It may also be the case that the Group is declined permission and must incur costs to return the building to its previous state. In terms of reporting non-compliance to the relevant regulatory authorities,ISA 250 requires the auditor to determine whether they have a responsibility to report the identified or suspected non-compliance to parties outside the entity.In the event management or those charged with governance of the Group fail to make the necessary disclosures to the regulatory authorities, Broadway &Co should consider whether they should make the disclosure.This will depend on matters including whether there is a legal duty to disclose or whether it is considered to be in the public interest to do so.As retrospective permission has been requested for the building upgrades,it is possible the Group has already notified relevant authorities,however,Broadway &Co will still need to consider if any unreported non-compliance issues remain. The IESBA International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (the Code)requires auditors to comply with the principle of confidentiality,and if disclosure was to be made by Broadway &Co,it would be advisable to seek legal advice on the matter. Further advice on disclosure in the public interest is given in the Code,which gives examples of situations where disclosure might be appropriate.The Code also clarifies that in exceptional circumstances where the auditor believes there may be an imminent breach of a law or regulation,they may need to disclose the matter immediately to an appropriate authority.Thee decision to disclose will always be a matter for the auditor's judgement and where the disclosure is made in good faith,it will not constitute a breach of the duty of confidentiality. 2 (a)ED 5000 and double materiality Aim of new proposed standard Part of the aim of the new proposed International Standard on Sustainability Assurance 5000(ED 5000)is to respond to the need for a standard which supports the consistent performance of quality sustainability assurance engagements which can be applied across all sustainability topics and reporting frameworks. Concept of double materiality When developing the proposed standard(ED 5000),the IAASB has considered the concept of double materiality in the preparation of sustainability information.This means there is a consideration of the significance of the impact of sustainability matters on the organisation,as well as the significance of the impacts of the business activity on the outside world.Some stakeholders will be interested in the financial impact of sustainability matters on the entity (often referred to as financial materiality),while others will be concerned about how the entity impacts on sustainability matters (referred to as impact materiality). ED 5000 considers this requirement when providing assurance on that sustainability information,to ensure the assurance provided can adequately assist the decision-making process of a wide range of users. Where the concept of double materiality is relevant,the assurance practitioner should consider both financial and impact materiality when determining their materiality level for the purposes of planning and performing the engagement.It should be noted that the IAASB's view is that it will not be relevant to every engagement. ED 5000 requires that the assurance practitioner should adopt a different approach for quantitative and qualitative disclosures and the IAASB has offered additional guidance on how to determine materiality where both financial and impact materiality apply: Quantitative disclosures For quantitative disclosures,materiality relates to the magnitude of misstatements relative to the disclosures and similar to other assurance engagements may be determined by applying a percentage or benchmark to the reported metric.The practitioner should also set a performance materiality level to minimise the risk of the aggregate of misstatements exceeding the materiality level.The IAASB states that where double materiality is relevant,the lesser of financial or impact materiality should be used as that would be the level which is material to the intended users or other stakeholders. Qualitative disclosures Qualitative disclosures by their very nature will be more subjective and a key consideration for the assurance practitioner is whether the disclosures are accurate and complete.Assurance practitioners need to consider whether the disclosures omit information which is pertinent to user understanding.They should also assess whether they include information which could obscure the presentation of the disclosures. The practitioner should also consider a range of other factors such as whether a potential misstatement could affect compliance with law or regulation,or the overall number of people or stakeholders affected by the matter.ED 5000 offers detailed guidance over the matters which should be considered when determining materiality for qualitative disclosures. Conclusion Embedding the concept of double materiality into ED 5000 should ensure that the assurance practitioner appropriately considers the information needs of the intended users and give more robust assurance on the suitability of the reporting criteria which have been selected by the reporting entity. (b)Fizzizoda Co-quality and professional issues Scope of the engagement The scope of the engagement needs to be agreed and it is imperative that the assurance provider makes it clear where there are limitations in the scope of their work and assurance. The letter of engagement should define the scope of the assurance engagement including the subject matter for review and thee benchmarks which will be used to evaluate it.Oak&Co will also need to verify the terms of the engagement whereby whether it will be a limited assurance or reasonable assurance engagement,and to plan the engagement procedures to meet the sufficient appropriate evidence threshold in each case.Oak&Co willneed to confirm who will be the users of the report,whether it will be for public use or to provide internal clarification of performance or attainment. As this is a separate engagement from the statutory audit,it should be stated in the report the extent of the limitation of their conclusions. Sufficient appropriate evidence When considering whether to accept an engagement,assurance practitioners should assess the level of risk involved.By assessing an entity's monitoring controls of the subject matter by those charged with governance,as well as confirming thee source and reliability of the evidence required to complete the engagement. Assurance practitioners will need to adopt additional professional scepticism and judgement,especially in areas of estimation or where the subject matter is reliant on third parties.For example,there must be an assessment of the nature and complexity of the underlying subject matter,and whether there is a high degree of uncertainty in its measurement.Additional substantive testing may be required as well in addition to the use of an independent expert. There should be adequate time and resources allowed in the assurance planning and engagement operation commensurate with the complexity of the subject matter and the controls of the company. This will enable the practitioner to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence to conclude on the engagement. Risk of management bias There is the increased risk of management bias as use of the RGW certificate is dependent on the incremental improvement in recycling rates each year.It appears that management has attempted to disguise this apparent decrease in recycling by thee change of metric and presentation of the sustainability report.This also raises the point of the integrity of management being questioned as there may be additional data or information which has not been disclosed by the client.Therefore,Oak &Co must ensure that they undertake robust testing and ensure that potential areas of estimation and subjectivity are assessed closely for further evidence of management manipulation of the data. This type of management bias may also be referred to as 'greenwashing’,whereby the information,which is chosen to be presented,the measurement tools used and the manner in which it is presented may be adapted by management in order to present the most opportune position.This is a significant potential problem,as an organisation which makes such false or misleading statements about sustainability information may be considered to be fraudulently reporting.Therefore,there is a higher risk of error,as well as deliberate misstatement,in the sustainability information which is published.This is particularly an issue as Fizzizoda Co has changed the way they present some of their data in the sustainability report,namely the botles which have been recycled.This may be a deliberate attempt to obscure the year-on-year changes. Professional expertise and external experts ED 5000 highlights the importance of both firm-level and engagement-level quality management,stating that the engagement leader shall take overall responsibility for managing and achieving quality on the engagement and also requiring that thee engagement leader must have competence and capabilities in assurance skills and techniques developed through extensive training and practical application. Oak &Co has sustainability assurance expertise within the firm which is evidenced by the existence of a separate team which specialises in this type of assurance.However,they still may need to engage an independent expert in plastic recycling and waste measurement to ensure that the subject matter they are reporting on is reliable.As Fizzizoda Co is not required to comply with specific reporting requirements,it is likely that the benchmarks are internal ones developed by the business or ones used within the drinks bottle industry.If these are external industry specific benchmarks,then the assurance team must ensure they have familiarity of them and how they are applied and any particular issues with their measurement or presentation requirements. If the benchmarks and measurement tools are internally developed by Fizzizoda Co,then the assurance team will need to apply professional judgement during their discussions with management as to whether they are reasonable. Users of the report Auditors need to understand how risks,such as those relating to the sustainability and recycling information,relate to their responsibilities under professional standards,and applicable laws and regulation.In particular,the assurance team need to understand the extent of reliance on their report by other stakeholders,especially RGW and the planned financing from Greentree Bank. There is potentially a reputational issue if Oak &Co produces a favourable report because of a lack of expertise in this area, and then Fizzizoda Co is later found to have breached the terms of its agreement with RGW's key non-financial indicators.This may be compounded if Greentree Bank subsequently provides finance to Fizzizoda Co based on the sustainability report and resulting assurance provided by Oak &Co. As Fizzizoda Co is facing increasing competition from an environmentally aware competitor,there is the increased risk of management bias due to pressure for the company to respond and rebuild some of the lost market share.This may potentially result in inflated recycling information in the sustainability report.Oak &Co may need to consider these longer term or business specific impacts,such as there may be cash flow implications if the report is used to support the planned additional financing from Greentree Bank or the potential to impact future investors or affect future investment or marketing strategies.The loss of the RGW certification would also affect financing from Greentree Bank. Auditors need to consider that the information in the sustainability report may be as a response to the increased competition the company is facing,and so it may be subject to manipulation by management. Tutorial notes: Credit will be given where potential loss of the audit if the assurance engagement fails to give Fizzizoda Co's management the RGW certification assurance they require to retain its logo. Candidates may adopt the acceptance guidance as set out in ED5000,however,in this question both the ISQM 1 and the ED5000 information has been incorporated. (c)Critique and application of professional scepticism The number of bottles of LiftUp sold during the year has increased by 12·4%,it would be reasonable,therefore,to expect an increase in the donations towards recycling to support the increase in botles in the environment.However,the donations have decreased by 5·4%($13 million)year on year.This may be caused by alternative methods of recycling,but LifUp bottles are only recycled using the subsidised network as stated in the sustainability report.The assurance team will need to understand why this has happened,and whether there are other forms of collection for recycling which are not mentioned in the report or whether there is a decrease in recycling.It suggests that the company is investing less in their recycling partner which supports the point that there may be less recycled material being used in the manufacture of their bottles. This latter point may be explained by the recycling metrics included in the report.Due to the change in presentation of thee information on recycling undertaken by local partners,it is not immediately clear that the claim that recycling has increased in 20X5 is verified,as stated in the notes to the sustainability report.The assurance team should apply a high degree of professional scepticism as this may be a simple omission on the part of the directors (providing no comparative figure)or it may be a deliberate attempt to disguise adverse reporting.This change of metric with little explanation in the notes to thee sustainability report suggests that this may be a deliberate manipulation of the data to conceal the decrease in recycling rates.The incentive for the directors would be to maintain their RGW certification and to appease shareholders and other stakeholders. The sustainability report states that the percentage of bottles sold which are made with recycled material is 23%(20X4:14%). However,the cost of recycled material purchased by Fizzizoda Co would contribute towards manufacturing only 18%of thee bottles,not the 23%stated in the sustainability report.The audit work has verified minimal fluctuation in the cost of materials, so this difference cannot be explained by a price fluctuation.Given that material costs have remained stable year on year,this suggests that the amount of new materials used by volume,as a proportion of the bottles,has not changed($0·21 per bottle). As part of the assurance review,analytical procedures will be used to assess the verifiability of the recycling figures stated in thee sustainability report:the report states that 3·3%of bottles were recycled by subsidised recycling partners in 20X4,this would amount to 452·1 million bottles.This recycling is reduced in 20X5 to 415·8 million bottles or 2·7%of total bottles,which is actually a decrease year on year.This does not take into account opening or closing inventory of bottles,but it does highlight potential inconsistencies which the directors would need to explain.This would suggest that the statements included in thee sustainability report in relation to the improvement in the key non-financial indicators is potentially untrue and also likely to be very misleading to the users of the report.The apparent decrease in grants and donations to support the recycling partners would also support this observation. The reluctance by the operational manager to provide additional supporting evidence for the recycling rates should also be treated with professional scepticism.The assurance team will need to use other methods of verifying whether the figures provided in the sustainability statement are consistent with the financial information of Fizzizoda Co. Any such issues should also be escalated to those charged with governance to ensure that the sustainability assurance team can obtain sufficient appropriate evidence. 3 (a)Data analytics discussion Data analytics is the use of automated tools and techniques to analyse data and draw conclusions.These tools are increasingly being used during external audit to analyse large volumes of client data and present it in understandable ways.The introduction of such tools,however,is not without challenges some of which are discussed in more detail below. Consistency and expectations of data analytics tools One of the key issues is that there is still a lack of consistency or a widely accepted standard across all firms in relation to thee use of data analytics.This results in dificulty establishing quality standards relating to the adoption of such tools and can be difficult for clients and the users of financial statements to assess the use of these tools across different audit firms. The use of data analytics may create an expectation gap among stakeholders who think that because the auditor is testing 100%of transactions in a specific area,the client's data must be 100%correct.This may lead to unrealistic expectations being placed on the auditor in relation to the detection of fraud and error. Use of and quality of data There are also challenges concerning data privacy and confidentiality.There is a risk confidential client data may be misused or lost which can expose the audit firm to serious legal and reputational consequences.Audit firms must ensure robust data security measures to preventillegal access which would affect both the legal and reputational status of the audit firm.They will need to ensure that they understand the system which produces the data in order to ensure that the results produced by thee tools are reliable. The quality of the data used in data analytics is crucial to the accuracy of the results Data can be inaccurate or incomplete for a variety of reasons,such as human error,system errors,or manipulation.Auditors should carefully assessthe quality of the data.This includes verifying the accuracy and completeness of the data and identifying any potential sources of manipulation. Further,not allclients have information organised in such a way forit to be possible for the data to be interrogated by the audit firms'data analysis tools.Data may not be extracted fully if the client system does not integrate properly with the audit firm's data analytic tool leading to issues over the completeness and integrity of the data used for audit testing. Some client companies may be reluctant to provide the necessary data or provide the audit firm with sufficient access to their systems,in order to carry out data analytics.This may be due to security concerns and while auditors could seek client permission,it could be time consuming or refused.If auditors are unable to efficiently obtain company data,they may not be able to use data analytics or may be reluctant to do so as it is not cost efficient or does not provide sufficient audit evidence. There may also be system incompatibility issues with the analytics tool and the client data system,preventing accurate or complete data sets. Audit staff expertise Audit staff may not understand the data analysis sufficiently to draw the correct conclusion or to know when sufficient evidence has been obtained.The results of data analytics are often presented in graphical formats,such as charts and graphs.This can be helpful for understanding the data,but it can also make it more dificult to interpret the results.Interpretation of the results of data analytics testing is important to identify potential outliers,issues in the results and ensure that the correct parameters are set for the specific assertions being tested.It is likely that experienced audit staff with knowledge of analysis of data analytic results would be required to effectively utilise the results from the data analytics and apply these results effectively to the audit plan and any further actions or analysis which is required. Different industries will have different requirements,and it is important to remember that the use of data analytics software is not a'one size fits all.Evidence on the audit file may not be complete if allthe detail of how a procedure was performed using the tool is not properly understood and documented. Evidence retention may also be dificult as large volumes of data must be held in such a way as to be accessible and useable for several years after an audit and the firm may not have sufficient data storage resources to meet this requirement. Data analytics has the ability to improve audit quality and to reduce the expectation gap by analysing more client data and identifying more risks,however,there are several practical issues which need to be addressed in terms of industry consistency, availability,training and safeguards before its full potential can be achieved. Tutorial note: Credit ill be given to other points which are reasonable,such as practical applications or timing of the use of the data tools in the audit plan process. (b)Bodeen Co-post issuance review Use of data analytics during the audit As Bodeen Co is a new and listed client,there is an increased level of audit risk arising from the lack of knowledge of thee business and its systems and controls.It is important that Burton &Co minimises detection risk through more detailed audit procedures.The use of data analytics is not a substitute for the need to carry out such detailed procedures and the audit must still be carried out to the required standard to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence that the financial statements are free from material misstatement.As this is a new client,the audit team should have documented their understanding of the systems and controls in place.The IT system which produces the data being analysed may be deficient either in its nature or how the data is input and stored. There appears to be a significant deficiency in the evidence obtained over revenue.It is not possible from a review of thee file to understand how the evidence was obtained and how the output of the data analytics testing demonstrated assertions regarding the financial statements.The documentation available is insufficient and is not enough to demonstrate that suficient appropriate evidence has been obtained without referring back to the underlying data.It is important to note that the data analytics tool alone would not be sufficient to test the completeness and accuracy of the Bodeen Co revenue.As this is a new client,and therefore there is the potential for there to be gaps in the understanding of the trends in revenue,and equally,where there may be adverse or unusual trends.The risk that there may be inappropriate conclusions raised based on the audit work completed is therefore increased. Tutorial note: Relevant points out of ISA 230 Audit Documentation will be awarded credit. There is a significant lack of understanding of the data analytics tool by the audit team.When new technologies or approaches are brought into the audit process,it is important for the audit team to fully understand their purpose and how to apply them. It may be that the audit team felt obliged to utilise the data analytics tool due to Bodeen Co's audit committee being supportive of its use.However,the team should be trained in its use and monitored for correct application to the audit performed. There also appears to be a lack of appropriate supervision and review of the work performed by the audit team.The fact that these issues in relation to revenue have not been identified during file review or rectified by the manager or partner prior to thee issuance of the auditor's report highlights a competency deficiency within the firm.More concerning is the fact that this might imply that the misunderstanding of the use of the data analytics software is prevalent at all levels of the audit team.This is further supported by the fact that issues were also highlighted by the engagement quality reviewer(EQR)but not addressed. The failure to use the appropriate specialists,which was specified in the audit strategy,represents a significant failure of quality management within the firm and suggests that there is a distinct lack of oversight over changes or deviations to a confirmed audit strategy. Overall,the audit team appears to have placed excessive reliance on the data analytics software without properly evaluating thee evidence obtained and whether it is both sufficient and appropriate.On this occasion,the audit opinion does not appear to be incorrect but there is a risk that the lack of understanding and documented evidence may be present in other audits performed by teams inexperienced in the use of the data analytics tool,and this should be investigated further. Engagement quality review The performance of the engagement quality review of the audit file prior to issuance is an example of good practice and is also required by ISA 220(Revised)Quality Management for an Audit of Financial Statements for listed clients. The issue raised by the engagement quality reviewer (EQR)regarding opening balances highlights there was insufficient audit evidence obtained in this regard,and this is a sign the review process can identify areas of concern in the audit. Simply accepting prior year financial statement figures as correct is inappropriate.Audit firms are expected to perform additional testing to ensure the accounting policies used in previous years are appropriate and the balances are not materially misstated. Without this testing,there is a risk of a material misstatement which would impact the curent year financial statements,and the opinion issued may be incorrect. There is a quality management failure with regards to the engagement quality review given the audit engagement partner has issued an unmodified audit opinion before the issues raised by the EQR had been addressed.The fact the audit engagement partner acknowledged insufficient work had been performed yet the report was still issued is very concerning and may suggest a lack of integrity.This is because they are seemingly comfortable with being associated with an auditor's report where there are omissions in the work. Actions Audits of other clients by the manager and partner responsible for the audit of Bodeen Co should be reviewed to ensure that these issues are not present in any more of their client audits. The firm should provide more in-depth training to their staff on the use of the data analytics software and how the output should be documented and assessed. A review by a technical specialist in the use of data analytics should be included as part of the audit process where thee data analytics tool is to be employed and controls should be established to ensure that this cannot be overridden. The resolution process for finalising auditor's reports where the EQR is not in agreement with the audit engagement partner,and reinforcement of the process for signing the auditor's report should be improved or implemented. Tutorial note: Other relevant actions will be given credit. (c)Evaluation of the proposed auditor's report-Frescia Co First,the titles of the paragraphs are incorrect.If the opinion is being qualified on the basis of a material,but not pervasive,lackk of evidence or misstatement,then the title would be 'Qualified Opinion'.The 'Basis for Limitation of Scope'paragraph should be titled 'Basis for Qualified Opinion'. There is no reference in the basis for qualified opinion paragraph to the relevant financial reporting standards or the impact of the impairment amount on elements of the financial statements.There is also no reference to the ethical standards governing the audit.The paragraph "We have conducted our audi...'should also make reference to fulfilment of the relevant ethical responsibilities of the auditor. The wordings used are unprofessional or inappropriate for several reasons: The opinion paragraph does not give an opinion-it simply states the accounts are true and fair-it should include thee phrase ‘in our opinion..' The auditor's report should not be referring to management's refusal'to adjust the financial statements-this personalises the issue. The reasons for the limitation of scope should be explained and the auditor's report should also not use the term 'prove'as this implies a higher level of assurance than can be provided. Appropriateness of the proposed opinion The misstatement of $6 million identified by the auditor is material based on the materiality threshold of $4·85 million. Although reference has been made to the impairment the auditors identified,it does not explicitly state the impact on thee statement of comprehensive income or statement of financial position.This is not clear enough for the users of the financial statements as they are left to calculate or guess the impact themselves. Burton &Co disagrees with management on the assumptions used in calculating the impairment of the magazine titles.This would therefore constitute a material misstatement rather than an inability to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence.Thee disagreement relates to a difference in judgement over these assumptions.This is much harder for the auditor to justify to management than factual misstatements;ISA 450 Evaluation of Misstatements Identified during the Audit states that in thee case of a judgemental misstatement,there is likely to be more discussion with management.The auditor will need to present their conclusion based on robust audit evidence,in order to explain the misstatement which has been uncovered and justify a recommended correction of the misstatement. In this case,the auditor appears to have performed their calculations on a worst-case basis and management on a best-case basis.The value of the titles is likely to lie somewhere between the two estimates.While it is not possible with certainty to say what the value in use of the titles would be,the auditor should explore a range of likely outcomes and determine whether a material misstatement still exists on the basis of the most likely assumptions. First time acquisition Breach of funding covenants and withdrawal of finance Laws and regulations Failure to attract quality productions Guarantees Government grants Maximum marks (b)Significant risks of material misstatement Generally,up to 3 marks for each risk evaluated unless otherwise indicated. Q1 A Suggested solution -Thornham Textiles Co Briefing notes To:Wendy Runton,audit engagement partner From:Audit manager Subject:Thornham Textiles Co-audit planning Introduction These briefing notes have been prepared to assist in planning the audit of new audit client,Thornham Textiles Co.The briefing notes contain an evaluation of the significant audit risks which should be considered in planning.These notes recommend the audit procedures with respect to the assets held for sale at the company and the new share-based payment scheme.Finally,they consider the factors to be considered when evaluating the nature and extent of reliance which can be placed on the internal audit department of Thornham Textiles Co. (b)Evaluation and prioritisation of significant audit risks Materiality 5-10%of profit before tax =range of $440,000-$880,000. This benchmark is only a starting point for determining planning materiality and professional judgement will need to be applied in determining a final level to be applied during the course of the audit.This is a new client and we are not familiar with the internal controls and systems at Thornham Textiles Co.Significant changes have taken place during the year such as setting up the new operation in Farland and litigation against three retailers.Further changes are planned with the closure of the Holt factory due to take place in November 20X5.Therefore,materiality has been based on 5%of profit before tax and has been set at $440,000. New client This is the first year that Sheringham Associates has audited the company which increases detection risk,as our firm does not have experience with the client,making it more difficult to detect material misstatements. In addition,there is a risk that opening balances and comparative information may not be correct.However,because the prior year figures were not audited by Sheringham Associates,we should plan to audit the opening balances carefully,in accordance with ISA 510 Initial Audit Engagements-Opening Balances,to ensure that opening balances and comparative information are both free from material misstatement. Analytical review The company is a listed entity,and the results of the preliminary analytical procedures carried out by the audit team indicate that there is a significant growth in revenue of 38.5%along with growth in profits of 35.4%.There will be pressure from shareholders for the company to return a better performance which creates an incentive for management bias,especially given the significant proposed changes in strategy.This means that management may use earnings management techniques, or other methods of creative accounting,to create a healthier picture of financial performance than is actually the case.This creates an inherent risk of material misstatement,at the financial statement level. The audit team should apply professional scepticism to areas of the audit where the accounting treatment is subject to management's judgement,especially if that treatment results in the acceleration or inflation of revenue,or the deferral of expenditure. Share-based payment scheme Thornham Textiles Co has introduced a share option scheme in the financial year.In accordance with IFRS 2 Share-based Payments,such transactions must be recognised as an increase in equity with a corresponding expense over the vesting period.The total expense recognised will be calculated as the number of options expected to vest multiplied by the fair value of the options at the grant date.This is then allocated over the vesting period,in this case three years. The expense for the financial year ending 30 September 20X5 will be $481,667, ([850 staff-X1,000 options x£1.70]/3).This amount is material. It is possible that management's assumptions regarding staff turnover may be incorrect,leading to errors in the calculation.As a new item in the financial statements,it would be considered a significant audit risk due to the estimations involved and the assumptions made by management. If management fails to recognise the accounting impact of the share-based payment scheme,then equity and expenses would be understated.Further,if management does not recognise sufficient staff turnover in their calculations,it will be the case that equity and expenses would be overstated. Tutorial note: Additional credit will be given for candidates linking that the redundancy payments might require amounts to be included for the loss of the share options and questioning the need for an increase in the provision if it has not already been included with calculations. Assets held for sale-classification The company plans on closing the Holt factory and selling either individual assets or the millas an ongoing business.The carrying amount of the assets to be sold is $4.7million and is therefore material to the statement of financial position. These items therefore are likely to fall within the scope of IFRS 5 Non-current Assets Held For Sale and Discontinued Operations.In general,the following conditions must be met for an asset (or 'disposal group')to be classified as held for sale: · management is committed to a plan to sell; ● the asset is available for immediate sale; · an active programme to locate a buyer is initiated; ·the sale is highly probable,within 12 months of classification as held for sale (subject to limited exceptions); · the asset is being actively marketed for sale at a sales price reasonable in relation to its fair value; · actions required to complete the plan indicate that it is unlikely that plan will be significantly changed or withdrawn. From the discussion with the audit committee,it would appear that the company is actively trying to find a buyer as it has been in negotiations to sell the mill as an ongoing business. factory as held for sale depending on whether the IFRS 5 criteria have been met byThere is a risk of material misstatement in relation to the classification of the Holt factory as held for sale depending on whether the IFRS 5 criteria have been met by the year end.If the classification is not appropriate,then there is a risk of incorrect the year end.If the classification is not appropriate,then there is a risk of incorrect presentation and disclosure in the financial statements in relation to the Holt factory, for example,the assets and liabilities of a disposal group should be separately presented in the statement of financial position. Assets held for sale-measurement In accordance with IFRS 5,immediately prior to classifying an asset or disposal group as held for sale,an impairment review is performed in accordance with the applicable IFRS Standards.Any impairment loss is recognised in profit or loss.After classification as held for sale,an additional impairment loss based on the difference between the adjusted carrying amounts of the asset/disposal group and fair value less costs to sell is recognised.Assets classified as held for sale are not subject to depreciation. The assets should be valued at the lower of carrying amount and recoverable amount where the recoverable amount is the higher of value in use and fair value less costs of disposal.The carrying amount of the assets in the disposal group is $4.7 million and the estimated $4 million negotiation amount would be the expected fair value.As the staff are being made redundant and production wound down at the mill,value in use is unlikely to be a significant amount,hence it would appear that the assets of the disposal group are impaired by $0.7 million,which is material.It is also a non-routine transaction which further increases audit risk. If management does not write down the Holt factory to its recoverable amount,then assets would be overstated and profits overstated. Redundancy provision The company is planning to make 150 staff redundant with the closure of the Holt factory.The cost of the redundancy at $1.2 million is a material amount. In accordance with IAS 37 Provisions,Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets, an entity must recognise a provision if,and only if: · a present obligation (legal or constructive)has arisen as a result of a past event (the obligating event), · payment is probable ('more likely than not'),and ·the amount can be estimated reliably. In the case of the redundancy payments expected to be paid in November 20X5,a provision would be required if there was a constructive or legal obligation to make the redundancy payments.Such an obligation would exist if management has made the employees aware of the plan and begun negotiations over the amounts of redundancy pay. The figure quoted by the audit committee may represent an estimate of the amounts and therefore is of a judgemental nature and may or may not take into account the implication of the share-based payment scheme or the potential transfer of 75 staff to the potential buyer interested in purchasing the Holt factory.As such the figure is susceptible to errors and is high risk. The provision could be as high as the stated total cost of $1.2million,or potentially reduced to $600,000(75/150×1.2million) If the provision for redundancy costs is not sufficient,then the assets and profits would be overstated,and the expenses understated for the year ending 30 September 20X5. Farland Mill While the company routinely sells internationally and is likely to have strong systems and controls over its sales,the opening of a new mill in Farland and the purchase of assets internationally introduces new foreign currency transactions and balances outside of the day-to-day sales system.In particular,the company has purchased property plant and machinery worth 14 million Pobbles.This translates to $6.3 million at the date of purchase and is material to the statement of financial position. According to IAS 21 The Effects of Changes in Foreign Exchange Rates,a foreign branch would account for the transactions in its own functional currency throughout the financial period and then its financial statements would be translated into the presentation currency of the company for inclusion in the financial statements of the company as a whole.In order to do this,the statement of profit or loss is translated at the average rate for the year and the statement of financial position at the year- end rate.This would mean that the Farland branch would prepare its own accounts in Pobbles and these would be translated into dollars when incorporated into Thornham Textiles Co's financial statements. As management has no experience with international acquisitions or operations,it is possible they either do not have sufficient accounting knowledge or appropriate systems in place to capture the correct information,hence this is a significant audit risk. Incorrect exchange rates at conversion or incorrect use of the foreign exchange rules for translation of individual transactions and balances would mean that the profits and assets of the company could be under or overstated depending on the direction of currency movements at the period end. Inventory Inventories have been damaged due to a severe flood and therefore may not be saleable.IAS 2 Inventories requires inventory to be held at the lower of cost and net realisable value.Management is estimating the damage at $625,000 hence there may need to be a write down of damaged items,and this is a material amount. Failure to write down damaged assets would result in overstatement of inventory and understatement of costs. As the flooding also affected the warehouse,there are likely to be additional costs to make this dry and watertight again.Therefore,the audit plan must ensure that sufficient attention is paid to any costs incurred prior to the year end. Litigation It appears that Thornham Textiles Co is involved in litigation against certain retailers. The company are seeking damages from the three companies of $2 million which is material. This potentially gives rise to a contingent asset for any damages awarded.IAS 37 requires that a contingent asset which is virtually certain is recognised as an asset of the company and that a contingent asset which is probable is disclosed in the notes to the financial statements.Failure to properly account for the contingent asset could result in either under or overstatement of assets and profits depending on the likelihood that the claim will be successful. There is also a risk of inadequate disclosure of the litigation in the notes to the financial statements. Conclusion Given the audit issues stated above,the audit team should focus on the areas where management judgement and estimation may materially affect the financial statements.Therefore,the audit team should focus on the sale of Holt factory as this is material both quantitatively (at $4.7 million)and qualitatively as it will affect the presentation and the users'understanding of the financial statements.As this is a new client,and there are several changes proposed to the business,it is important that the audit team gains a thorough knowledge of the internal controls at the business,as well as understanding the accounting policies,in particular,in respect of judgemental areas such as provisions and classification of the Holt factory sale. (b)Audit procedures (i)Audit procedures in respect of the non-current assets relating to the planned closure of the Holt Factory Obtain a list of the assets relating to the Holt factory and agree to the non-current asset register to confirm carrying amount. Discuss with management the stage of progress of negotiations with the potential buyer and whether there is any interest from other parties as evidence that the sale of the mill as an operating unit is likely to occur. Review correspondence with the potential buyer for the millto assess the progress of negotiations and for evidence of the purchase price. - Obtain management's impairment review and assess whether the assumptions are in line with the auditor's understanding. Cast management's impairment review for arithmetical accuracy. Obtain the most recent surveyor's report for the Holt factory property to confirm the likely net realisable value for the property as an individual asset. Obtain current market prices for used mill equipment from the trade press or a specialist valuer to identify realisable value of the machinery. Confirm that impairment losses are correctly allocated to the assets of the unit by evaluating management's method for determining the impairment loss. Review board minutes for evidence the sale has been approved and to understand the progress of negotiations. occurred in the statement of financial position and appropriate disclosuresReview the financial statements to ensure reclassification as held for sale has occurred in the statement of financial position and appropriate disclosures regarding the proposed sale have been disclosed in the notes to the financial regarding the proposed sale have been disclosed in the notes to the financial statements. Obtain a written representation from management that it is their intention to complete the sale of the Holt factory within 12 months of the classification as assets held for sale. Review the components of the total depreciation charge for the year to confirm that no depreciation has been charged after reclassification as assets held for sale. (ii)Audit procedures in respect of the share-based payment scheme - Obtain a copy of the terms and conditions of the share-based payment scheme to confirm the vesting conditions and the number of shares and prices involved. Identify any terms in the agreement regarding redundancy or transfer of employment. If necessary,seek legal clarification regarding the staff of the Holt factory and their entitlement to retain their share options. Obtain board minutes to confirm approval of the share-based payment scheme. Discuss with management how the fair value at the grant date and other assumptions have been performed and assess their basis for consistency with auditor's understanding. If an expert was used to provide the fair value of the options at the grant date,consider the independence and competence of the expert to assess the extent of reliance which can be placed on them. Discuss with Human Resources(HR)how many staff have been awarded share options and whether any of those staff have left the company. Discuss with HR the historical levels of staff turnover to assess the reasonableness of the assumption that no staff will leave during the vesting period. Review the calculation of the annual expense for the share options and ensure the completeness and accuracy of the calculation. (c)Matters which should be considered in determining the amount of reliance to be placed on Thornham Textiles Co's internal audit department According to ISA 610 Using the Work of Internal Auditors,the external auditor may decide to use the work of the audit client's internal audit function to modify the nature or timing,or reduce the extent,of audit procedures to be performed directly by the external auditor. In some jurisdictions the external auditor may be prohibited,or restricted to some extent,by law or regulation from using the work of the internal audit function. Therefore Sheringham Associates should consider whether it is prohibited by the law or regulations which it must adhere to from relying on the work of Thornham Textiles Co's internal audit department or using the internal auditors to provide direct assistance.However,at a minimum the external auditor can review the findings of the internal audit team's work on internal controls to obtain understanding and inform their evaluation of control risk.This will be especially useful given that this is the first year that Sheringham Associates has performed the audit of Thornham Textiles Co. Sheringham Associates must evaluate the internal audit department to determine whether its work is suitable by evaluating: - The extent to which the internal audit function's organisational status and relevant policies and procedures support the objectivity of the internal auditors; - The level of competence of the internal audit function;and Whether the internal audit function applies a systematic and disciplined approach,including quality control. In assessing the first of these requirements,one of the criteria is whether the internal audit team is objective.The internal auditor should be able to report their findings in an unbiased way without fear of repercussion from management.As such,it would be expected that the internal audit department reports directly to the audit committee. When assessing competence,the qualification and experience of the head of internal audit and the internal audit staff should be considered.It would be expected that the department has staff who are qualified accountants with internal audit experience.A programme of ongoing training and development would also be expected to ensure that the department is up-to-date with new developments in financial reporting and other issues.The resources available to the internal audit department will also impact on the competence of the department as a whole.An understaffed department is likely to be less effective than a department with sufficient resources to carry out the role. To assess the final requirement,Sheringham Associates would need to review the reports and findings of the internal audit department to assess whether the nature and quality of the documentation and the conclusions are appropriate.They would investigate what level of review and authorisation was performed on the findings and the process for planning the work to be performed.If the department is free to plan its own investigations rather than focus only on areas allowed by management and where management acts upon the findings,a higher level of reliance would be possible. Even when the requirements are all met,it is required that the external auditor makes all significant judgements in the audit engagement and it is expected that the higher the level of risk of misstatement an item involves,the lower the reliance on internal audit shall be. If the external auditor wishes to make use of direct assistance with procedures from the internal audit function,this cannot cover procedures which involve making significant judgements in the audit or relate to higher assessed risks of material misstatement where the judgement required in performing the relevant audit procedures or evaluating the audit evidence gathered is more than limited.In addition,the procedures cannot relate to work in which the internal auditors have already been involved. Marking Guide -Thornham Textiles Co- (a)Evaluation of audit risks Up to 3 marks for each audit risk evaluated unless otherwise indicated. In addition,2 mark for relevant trends or calculations which form part of the evaluation of business risk or risk of material misstatement (max 3 marks). Up to 3 additional marks for materiality,comprising the appropriate calculation of the lower and upper thresholds based on projected revenue -1 mark for each calculation,plus 1 mark for justification of materiality determined within the range. · New client-increased detection risk (2 marks) · Analytical review(2 marks) · Share-based payment (4 marks) ● Assets held for sale-classification ● Assets held for sale-measurement · Provision for redundancy(up to 4 marks) · Foreign exchange-opening of Farland Mill · Inventory · Litigation -contingent asset Maximum 23 marks (b)Audit procedures Generally,1 mark for each well-described procedure. Obtain a written representation from management that it is their intention to complete the sale of the Holt factory within 12 months of the classification as assets held for sale. Review the components of the total depreciation charge for the year to confirm that no depreciation has been charged after reclassification as assets held for sale. (ii) Share-based payment scheme Obtain a copy of the terms and conditions of the share-based payment scheme to confirm the vesting conditions and the number of shares and prices involved. Identify any terms in the agreement regarding redundancy or transfer of employment. If necessary,seek legal clarification regarding the staff of the Holt factory and their entitlement to retain their share options.
